docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This document is a structured, multi-chapter reference guide designed to help early-career and intermediate game developers build fluency in the language, terminology, and conceptual frameworks used across the modern game development industry.

It was created for members of the Spawn.co community who are actively learning, building, and collaborating on games but may not yet have full exposure to the professional vocabulary used in studios, production pipelines, and cross-disciplinary communication.

Game development is not only a technical or creative discipline — it is also a language problem.

Different teams (design, engineering, art, audio, production, QA, and business) often describe the same system in completely different ways. Misalignment in terminology is one of the most common causes of confusion, inefficiency, and design breakdown in real production environments.

This encyclopedia is designed to solve that gap.

docs.title: What This Resource Is
docs.text:
This is a structured language and concept system for game development.

It includes:

  • Core terminology used in real studios
  • Cross-disciplinary vocabulary (design, art, engineering, production, audio, UX, business)
  • Production pipeline language from pre-production to live operations
  • Industry slang and informal communication terms
  • Advanced mental models used by senior developers, directors, and producers
  • Cross-functional communication frameworks

Rather than focusing only on definitions, it explains how terminology is used in context and how it reflects deeper systems, workflows, and decision-making structures.

docs.title: How This Will Help You
docs.text: By studying this guide, you will be able to:

docs.title: 1. Communicate Like a Studio Developer
docs.text:
Understand and use professional terminology in:

  • Team meetings
  • Design discussions
  • Code reviews
  • Art critiques
  • Production planning sessions

docs.title: 2. Translate Between Disciplines
docs.text:
Understand how the same idea is interpreted differently by:

  • Designers
  • Engineers
  • Artists
  • Producers
  • Audio teams
  • QA teams

This reduces miscommunication and improves collaboration.

docs.title: 3. Understand Game Development as a System
docs.text:
Instead of seeing isolated features, you will begin to see:

  • Interconnected systems
  • Production pipelines
  • Risk structures
  • Player behavior loops
  • Economic and engagement systems

docs.title: The Structural Topology of a Living Game Ecology
docs.text: In professional development environments, a project is never treated as a series of flat features. It is built as a highly interdependent stack where every design decision triggers downstream technical constraints.

docs.title: 4. Think at a Senior Level Faster
docs.text:
The guide includes:

  • Mental models used by experienced developers
  • Production-aware thinking frameworks
  • Systems thinking approaches
  • Creative constraint reasoning

docs.title: 5. Prepare for Real Studio Environments
docs.text:
You will become familiar with:

  • Industry shorthand and jargon
  • Production workflows and pipelines
  • Live service terminology
  • Studio hierarchy and communication structure

docs.title: How to Use This Document
docs.text:
This is not meant to be read once.

It should be used as:

  • A reference manual during development work
  • A translation guide during collaboration
  • A study resource for structured learning
  • A language alignment tool for team communication

Recommended approach:

  1. Read one chapter at a time
  2. Revisit terms when encountering them in real projects
  3. Practice translating ideas between disciplines
  4. Apply terminology in design documents and discussions

docs.title: Closing Note
docs.text:
This encyclopedia is intended to grow with experience.

It does not replace real development work — it is meant to accelerate understanding of it.

The ultimate goal is not memorization of terms, but fluency in the language of making games: how systems interact, how teams communicate, and how ideas become playable experiences.

docs.title: Part I — Foundations of Game Development
Chapter 1: What Game Development Actually Is
docs.text:
Most beginners view game development as:

"Making a game."

Professionals view game development as:

"The coordinated production of an interactive software product that delivers intended player experiences under technical, financial, production, and market constraints."

This distinction is important.

docs.title: Pipeline vs. Network Thinking
docs.text:
Most beginners imagine game development as a linear, straightforward sequence of steps—a pipeline where one phase finishes and hands off cleanly to the next.

flowchart LR
    Idea --> Design
    Design --> Art
    Art --> Programming
    Programming --> Testing
    Testing --> Launch
    Launch --> Support
How beginners imagine game development.

In reality, professional studios operate in a highly interconnected network of teams, feedback loops, dependencies, and constraints. Game design, art, code, production, and QA are constantly reacting to, modifying, and limiting each other.

flowchart LR
    subgraph Management["Management & Planning"]
        Marketing["Marketing"] --> Production["Production"]
    end

    subgraph Core["Core Creation Loop"]
        Design["Design"] <--> Programming["Programming"]
        Art["Art"] <--> Programming
        Audio["Audio"] --> Design
        Audio --> Programming
    end

    subgraph Validation["Validation & Release"]
        QA["QA"]
    end

    Production --> Design
    Production --> Programming
    Production --> Art

    Programming --> QA
    Art --> QA
    QA --> Design
    QA --> Programming
    QA --> Art
    QA --> Production
How professional studios actually operate.

Because changes in one discipline create immediate ripple effects in others, game development is a networked web of relationships, not a one-way conveyor belt.

Senior developers do not think primarily in terms of:

  • Features
  • Art assets
  • Code

They think in terms of:

  • Systems
  • Constraints
  • Pipelines
  • Risks
  • Tradeoffs
  • User experience outcomes
mindmap
root(("Professional Thinking"))
    Systems
        Economy
        Progression
        Combat
    Constraints
        Budget
        Time
        Team
    Risk
        Technical
        Production
        Market
    Tradeoffs
        Quality
        Scope
        Speed
    Outcomes
        Retention
        Engagement
        Revenue

Senior developers think in terms of systems, constraints, risks, tradeoffs, and outcomes rather than individual implementation details. Every addition or change is evaluated by its systemic cost, how it affects production schedules, and the risk it introduces to the project.

docs.title: Layers of Understanding
docs.text:
Expertise in game development develops through a progression of mental models. As developers gain experience, their focus shifts from local components to systemic tradeoffs and strategic alignment.

flowchart TD
    Terms --> Concepts
    Concepts --> Systems
    Systems --> Interactions
    Interactions --> Tradeoffs
    Tradeoffs --> Strategy
    Strategy --> Leadership
  • Beginners focus on memorizing terminology (e.g., what is a draw call?).
  • Intermediate Developers understand concepts in isolation (e.g., how to reduce draw calls).
  • Senior Developers analyze systems and their dependencies (e.g., how UI design affects draw calls).
  • Leads evaluate the interactions and tradeoffs (e.g., whether to prioritize lower latency or higher visual fidelity).
  • Directors & Producers weigh the strategic implications and align teams across disciplines, translating technical limitations into creative solutions.

True expertise is not just about accumulating facts—it is about developing more sophisticated mental models of how games are built and played.

docs.title: The Five Layers of a Game
docs.text:
Every game can be understood as five interconnected layers:

PLAYER EXPERIENCE       ↑GAME DESIGN       ↑CONTENT       ↑TECHNOLOGY       ↑PRODUCTION

docs.title: Player Experience
docs.text:
The actual emotions and experiences players feel.

Examples:

  • Tension
  • Mastery
  • Discovery
  • Competition
  • Wonder
  • Fear

A player never directly experiences code.

They experience outcomes.

docs.title: Design Layer
docs.text:
Design creates:

  • Rules
  • Goals
  • Constraints
  • Choices

Examples:

  • Health systems
  • Economy systems
  • Skill trees
  • Combat mechanics

Design translates desired experiences into systems.

docs.title: Content Layer
docs.text:
Content is everything players consume:

  • Levels
  • Characters
  • Weapons
  • Animations
  • Quests
  • Dialogue

A common phrase:

"Design creates systems. Content fills systems."

docs.title: Technology Layer
docs.text:
Technology makes everything function.

Includes:

  • Rendering
  • Physics
  • Networking
  • AI
  • Audio
  • Tools

Programmers mostly operate here.

docs.title: Production Layer
docs.text:
Production coordinates everything.

Without production:

  • Deadlines fail
  • Budgets explode
  • Teams become blocked

Producers optimize information flow.

docs.title: Everything is Connected
docs.text:
Game development is the management of interconnected, dynamic systems. Adding or changing a single feature does not occur in a vacuum; it sends waves across multiple systems, affecting balance, visuals, performance, and player behavior.

graph TD
    Combat --> Economy
    Combat --> Progression
    Combat --> UI
    Combat --> Audio
    Combat --> Analytics
    Combat --> Difficulty
    Difficulty --> Retention
    Economy --> Retention
    Progression --> Retention

For example, adjusting the damage of a weapon (Combat) directly cascades to: - Difficulty & Retention: If combat is too hard or too easy, retention drops. - Progression & Economy: Players may earn resources faster or slower, distorting the progression loop. - UI & Audio: Visual telegraphs and audio hits must be updated to align with the new combat dynamics. - Analytics: Data tracking must monitor how this tweak shifts overall player behavior.

Viewing a game as a network of connected systems prevents the "silo effect," where one discipline implements a feature that inadvertently breaks another.

docs.title: The Game Studio as a System
docs.text:
Just as the game itself is a system of rules, the studio that creates it is a system of specialized, coordinated teams.

mindmap
root(("Game Studio"))
    Design
    Programming
    Art
    Audio
    Production
    QA
    Marketing
    Community
    Analytics

A successful game requires all parts of the studio to operate as a coherent organism: - Design designs systems. - Programming implements them. - Art and Audio fill them with sensory content. - Production plans resource allocations. - QA validates behavior. - Marketing, Community, and Analytics manage player interactions, expectations, and feedback loops.

docs.title: The Communication Problem
docs.text:
Because a game studio is composed of separate, specialized disciplines, communication issues are common. Different roles view the game through different lenses and speak separate technical languages, even when they are working on the exact same feature.

flowchart LR
    Designer["Game Feel"] --> PlayerExperience
    Programmer["Input Latency"] --> PlayerExperience
    Animator["Animation Timing"] --> PlayerExperience
    Audio["Response Feedback"] --> PlayerExperience
    PlayerExperience["Player Experience"]
  • A Designer wants to improve "Game Feel".
  • A Programmer optimizes "Input Latency".
  • An Animator adjusts "Animation Timing".
  • An Audio Designer tunes "Response Feedback".

All of them are describing the same target: Player Experience. This encyclopedia serves as a translation layer to help these disciplines align their vocabulary and understand each other's constraints.

docs.title: Why Projects Succeed or Fail
docs.text:
The quality of communication within a studio directly determines the quality of the final game. When communication breaks down, projects enter a negative feedback loop that often leads to failure.

flowchart LR
    PoorCommunication --> Misalignment
    Misalignment --> Rework
    Rework --> Delays
    Delays --> BudgetPressure
    BudgetPressure --> LowerQuality
    LowerQuality --> Failure

Conversely, establishing a shared vocabulary builds a positive loop of clear decisions and solid execution.

flowchart LR
    SharedVocabulary --> SharedUnderstanding
    SharedUnderstanding --> BetterDecisions
    BetterDecisions --> BetterExecution
    BetterExecution --> BetterGames

Shared understanding is not a luxury; it is one of the most valuable assets a development team can possess.

docs.title: Pre-Production
docs.text:
Goal:

Determine whether the game should exist.

Many beginners incorrectly think production begins with coding.

Professionals spend enormous effort before production begins.

docs.title: Concept Phase
docs.text:
Questions:

  • What is the game?
  • Who is it for?
  • Why will players care?

Deliverables:

  • High concept
  • Pitch deck
  • Market analysis
  • Initial vision

Common terminology:

docs.title: Elevator Pitch
docs.text:
One-sentence game summary.

Example:

"Dark Souls meets Pokémon in a procedurally generated ocean world."

docs.title: USP (Unique Selling Proposition)
docs.text:
The core differentiator.

Example:

Fully destructible voxel cities.

docs.title: Fantasy
docs.text:
Not genre.

A design term.

Fantasy means:

The role or experience the player imagines inhabiting.

Examples:

  • Space pirate
  • Master assassin
  • Elite commander

Many design decisions are judged by:

Does this reinforce the fantasy?

docs.title: Prototyping
docs.text:
Purpose:

Answer risk questions.

Not:

Build the game.

Instead:

Prove assumptions.

Questions:

  • Is combat fun?
  • Does movement work?
  • Can networking support this?

docs.title: Prototype
docs.text:
Fast.

Ugly.

Disposable.

Built for learning.

Not production quality.

docs.title: Spike
docs.text:
Programming term.

A temporary implementation used to investigate uncertainty.

Example:

Testing procedural terrain generation.

docs.title: Vertical Slice
docs.text:
One of the most misunderstood terms.

A Vertical Slice is:

A small section of the game built to final quality.

Not:

A prototype.

Not:

A demo.

A slice demonstrates:

  • Gameplay
  • Art
  • Audio
  • UX
  • Technology

working together.

Publishers often evaluate projects using vertical slices.

docs.title: Production
docs.text:
Production means:

Creating the complete game.

Now systems become:

  • Scalable
  • Maintainable
  • Shippable

docs.title: Alpha
docs.text:
Feature complete.

Major systems exist.

Not necessarily polished.

Question:

Does everything exist?

docs.title: Beta
docs.text:
Content complete.

Focus shifts toward:

  • Stability
  • Balance
  • Polish

Question:

Does everything work?

docs.title: Release Candidate (RC)
docs.text:
Potential shipping build.

Multiple RCs may exist.

Example:

RC1 RC2 RC3

Until one passes certification.

docs.title: Gold Master
docs.text:
Historically:

The final build sent for manufacturing.

Today:

The version approved for launch.

docs.title: Launch
docs.text:
Most beginners believe launch is the end.

Professionals know:

Launch is usually the beginning.

docs.title: Live Operations (LiveOps)
docs.text:
Continuous management after release.

Includes:

  • Events
  • Balancing
  • Analytics
  • Seasonal content
  • Community management

Games like:

  • Fortnite
  • Destiny
  • Warframe

are essentially LiveOps products.

docs.title: Mechanics
docs.text:
Definition:

Rules governing interaction.

Examples:

  • Jumping
  • Shooting
  • Crafting

Mechanics answer:

What can players do?

docs.title: Dynamics
docs.text:
Definition:

Behaviors emerging from mechanics.

Example:

Mechanic:

  • Trading

Dynamic:

  • Player economies

Players experience dynamics.

Designers create mechanics.

docs.title: Aesthetics (MDA Framework)
docs.text:
Aesthetic means:

Desired emotional outcome.

Examples:

  • Challenge
  • Discovery
  • Narrative
  • Competition

Professionals often work backwards:

Emotion→ Dynamic→ Mechanic

Not:

Mechanic→ Hope it is fun

docs.title: Systems Design
docs.text:
A system is:

Multiple mechanics interacting through feedback loops.

Example:

Combat→ Loot→ Progression→ Stronger Combat

Systems designers think primarily in loops.

docs.title: Feedback Loop
docs.text: A cycle where outputs influence future inputs.

docs.title: Positive Feedback Loop
docs.text:
Amplifies advantages.

Example:

Winning provides more resources.

Common in strategy games.

Danger:

Runaway leaders.

docs.title: Negative Feedback Loop
docs.text:
Helps trailing players recover.

Example:

Mario Kart rubber-banding.

Purpose:

Maintain competition.

docs.title: Core Loop
docs.text:
Most repeated player activity.

Example:

Fight→ Loot→ Upgrade→ Fight

A game's health often depends on the quality of its core loop.

docs.title: Meta Loop
docs.text:
Long-term progression layer.

Example:

Matches→ Unlocks→ New Builds→ More Matches

docs.title: Agency
docs.text:
One of the most important design concepts.

Agency:

Meaningful player influence over outcomes.

Not:

Freedom.

Players can have many options but little agency.

docs.title: Player Expression
docs.text:
Ability to personalize behavior.

Examples:

  • Character builds
  • Playstyles
  • Cosmetics

High-expression games:

  • Minecraft
  • Path of Exile
  • Baldur's Gate 3

docs.title: Skill Floor
docs.text: Minimum competence required.

docs.title: Skill Ceiling
docs.text:
Maximum mastery possible.

Professional designers often discuss:

Accessibility versus depth.

This is usually a skill floor/ceiling discussion.

docs.title: Chapter 4: Professional Design Language
docs.text:
When senior designers discuss a feature, they rarely say:

"Make it more fun."

Instead they use vocabulary like:

docs.title: Readability
docs.text: Can players understand information quickly?

docs.title: Clarity
docs.text: Is intended behavior obvious?

docs.title: Telegraphing
docs.text:
Communicating future events before they occur.

Example:

Boss wind-up animation.

docs.title: Affordance
docs.text:
Visual indication of possible interaction.

Example:

A ladder suggests climbing.

docs.title: Signifier
docs.text:
The cue communicating the affordance.

Example:

Yellow paint on climbable ledges.

docs.title: Onboarding
docs.text: Teaching players.

docs.title: Cognitive Load
docs.text: Mental effort required.

docs.title: Friction
docs.text:
Anything slowing player actions.

Sometimes bad.

Sometimes intentional.

docs.title: Juice
docs.text:
Polish that increases perceived responsiveness.

Examples:

  • Screen shake
  • Particles
  • Sound effects

Game designers often say:

"This mechanic needs more juice."

docs.title: The Most Important Shift From Beginner to Professional
docs.text:
Beginners think:

Features create fun.

Professionals think:

Systems create experiences.

Beginners ask:

"What feature should we add?"

Professionals ask:

"What player behavior are we trying to create?"

Beginners focus on:

  • Objects
  • Assets
  • Mechanics

Professionals focus on:

  • Interactions
  • Feedback loops
  • Incentives
  • Constraints
  • Emergent outcomes

That mental shift is one of the clearest indicators that someone is beginning to think like a senior game developer rather than simply someone who knows how to make games.

docs.title: Part II — Game Design Systems & Theory
docs.text: Game design systems are best understood visually. A system is not a list of features. It is a network of rules, resources, feedback loops, constraints, and player choices that produce behavior over time.

docs.title: Chapter 1: Systems Design
docs.text: Systems design is the discipline of creating rules, mechanics, and structures that interact dynamically to produce gameplay experiences. Unlike linear content design, systems design focuses on creating rules that govern a set of behaviors, allowing players to explore possibilities within those boundaries.

docs.title: System
docs.text: A collection of parts interacting according to a set of rules to form a unified whole. In game design, a system is composed of multiple mechanics working in tandem (e.g., a weapon system + physical collision system + health system).

docs.title: What A System Actually Is
docs.text:
A system is rarely a single mechanic. Most systems are collections of interconnected mechanics producing emergent outcomes.

flowchart TD
    Weapon["Weapon System"] --> Combat["Combat System"]
    Health["Health System"] --> Combat
    Collision["Collision System"] --> Combat
    AI["AI System"] --> Combat
    Combat --> Experience["Player Experience"]

A game system consists of multiple mechanical modules interacting dynamically. When these components connect, they produce a rich experience.

docs.title: Systems Thinking Hierarchy
docs.text:
Professional designers think in terms of interactions rather than individual mechanics.

flowchart TD
    Mechanics["Mechanics"] --> Systems["Systems"]
    Systems --> Interactions["Interactions"]
    Interactions --> Emergence["Emergence"]
    Emergence --> Experience["Player Experience"]

Progressing up this hierarchy shifts focus from single actions to systems, player actions, dynamic emergence, and emotional outcomes.

Designer takeaway

A mechanic becomes powerful when it connects meaningfully to other mechanics.

docs.title: Feedback Loop
docs.text: A cycle in which the output of a system is routed back as an input, influencing the system's subsequent behavior.

docs.title: Positive Feedback Loop
docs.text:
Positive loops accelerate advantages, creating momentum.

flowchart LR
    Kill["Kill Enemies"] --> XP["Gain XP"]
    XP --> Stronger["Become Stronger"]
    Stronger --> Kill

When players win, positive loops increase their capacity to win. This rewards success but can cause runaway leaders if unchecked.

docs.title: Negative Feedback Loop
docs.text:
Negative loops counter changes, creating stability and preventing runaway victories.

flowchart LR
    Behind["Player Falls Behind"] --> CatchUp["Catch-Up Mechanic"]
    CatchUp --> Reduced["Reduced Gap"]
    Reduced --> Balance["Match Balance"]

Trailing players receive benefits or leading players face hurdles, keeping the match tight and competitive.

Designer takeaway

Positive feedback creates momentum. Negative feedback creates stability.

docs.title: Chapter 2: Economy Design
docs.text: Economy design defines the flow of resources through a game. A balanced economy ensures that resources retain value and player choices remain meaningful.

docs.title: Faucets (Sources)
docs.text: The mechanisms through which resources are introduced into the game economy. - Examples: Completing quests (rewards currency), mining ore, looting chests.

docs.title: Sinks (Drains)
docs.text: The mechanisms through which resources are permanently removed from the game economy. - Examples: Repair costs for gear, buying cosmetic upgrades, tax on market transactions.

docs.title: Black Holes (Destruction)
docs.text: Mechanisms that consume high volumes of resources in exchange for temporary buffs or random chances. - Examples: Upgrading gear with a chance of failure/destruction.

docs.title: Resource Flow Model
docs.text:
Every game economy is fundamentally resource flow management.

flowchart LR
    Faucets["Faucets (Sources)"] --> Inventory["Player Inventory"]
    Inventory --> Sinks["Sinks (Drains)"]
    Inventory --> BlackHoles["Black Holes"]
    Sinks --> Removed["Removed From Play"]
    BlackHoles --> Destroyed["Destroyed / Upgraded"]

Designers must carefully balance inflow (faucets) and outflow (sinks/black holes) to prevent inflation or stagnation in player resources.

docs.title: Healthy Economy Model
docs.text:
Value emerges from scarcity and meaningful spending opportunities.

flowchart LR
    ResourceGeneration["Resource Generation"] --> ResourceCirculation["Resource Circulation"]
    ResourceCirculation --> ResourceSpending["Resource Spending"]
    ResourceSpending --> ResourceScarcity["Resource Scarcity"]
    ResourceScarcity --> ResourceValue["Resource Value"]

Resources maintain value when they are scarce and when spending opportunities are meaningful to player choices.

Designer takeaway

A resource only feels valuable when players have meaningful reasons to earn, spend, save, or risk it.

docs.title: Chapter 3: Progression Systems
docs.text: Progression systems govern how a player’s power, options, and status increase over time. Effective progression keeps players engaged by offering regular, satisfying milestones.

docs.title: Vertical Progression
docs.text: Linear power increases (e.g., higher damage numbers, more health, stronger gear). This type of progression is easy to measure but can make older content obsolete.

docs.title: Horizontal Progression
docs.text: Increasing options and utility without necessarily raising raw power (e.g., unlocking new skills, character classes, traversal techniques, or playstyles). This maintains the relevance of older content but is harder to balance.

docs.title: Vertical vs Horizontal Progression
docs.text:
Progression can increase power vertically or expand choices horizontally.

flowchart TD
    Prog["Progression"] --> Vert["Vertical Progression"]
    Prog --> Horiz["Horizontal Progression"]
    Vert --> Power["More Power"]
    Vert --> Numbers["Bigger Numbers"]
    Horiz --> Options["More Options"]
    Horiz --> Playstyles["New Playstyles"]

Vertical progression raises power levels but can render content obsolete. Horizontal progression adds new choices and playstyles without inflating base stats.

docs.title: Long-Term Engagement Model
docs.text:
Progression is fundamentally motivational design.

flowchart LR
    Goal["Goal Set"] --> Progress["Visible Progress"]
    Progress --> Reward["Satisfying Reward"]
    Reward --> Motivation["Renewed Motivation"]
    Motivation --> Goal

Progression is motivational design. Visible progress toward clear goals keeps players engaged.

Designer takeaway

Progression is not just power growth. It is motivation design.

docs.title: Chapter 4: Emergent Gameplay
docs.text: Emergent gameplay occurs when players use simple rules and systems in unexpected, creative combinations to produce unique situations.

docs.title: Systemic Autonomy
docs.text: Individual entities must behave according to consistent, independent rules rather than pre-scripted behaviors.

docs.title: Interconnectedness
docs.text: Different systems must be aware of and react to one another (e.g., fire ignites grass, wind spreads fire, rain extinguishes fire).

docs.title: Emergence Example
docs.text:
No individual rule creates emergence. Emergence occurs when multiple rules interact.

flowchart TD
    Fire["Fire System"] --> Grass["Grass Material"]
    Wind["Wind Direction"] --> Fire
    Rain["Rain System"] --> Fire
    Grass --> Wildfire["Wildfire Event"]

No single system controls the outcome. Simple rules (fire burns grass, wind carries fire, rain kills fire) combine to create unscripted emergent scenarios.

docs.title: Scripted vs Emergent
docs.text:
Understanding the difference between pre-defined linear triggers and open system dynamics.

flowchart LR
    Scripted["Scripted Event"] --> Predetermined["Predetermined Outcome"]
versus 
flowchart LR
    SysA["System A"] --> Out["Outcome"]
    SysB["System B"] --> Out
    SysC["System C"] --> Out
    Out --> Unpredictable["Unpredictable Situation"]

Scripted events lead to a single outcome. Systems combine dynamically to allow players to discover unscripted situations.

Designer takeaway

Emergence comes from simple rules interacting in surprising ways.

docs.title: Chapter 5: Balance Theory
docs.text: Balance theory covers the mathematical and psychological methodologies for adjusting game difficulty, reward rates, and competitive fairness.

docs.title: Symmetric Balance
docs.text: Both players start with identical tools and resources (e.g., Chess, classic multiplayer shooters). It is inherently fair but can lead to predictable matches.

docs.title: Symmetric Balance Model
docs.text:
In symmetric balance, players begin with identical resources and tools.

flowchart LR
    PlayerA["Player A"] --> Same["Same Tools & Rules"]
    PlayerB["Player B"] --> Same

Fairness is guaranteed because players start under identical conditions. Variety depends on strategy rather than tool differences.

docs.title: Asymmetric Balance
docs.text: Players start with different tools, factions, or abilities but have equivalent opportunities for victory (e.g., StarCraft, character-based shooters). It offers high variety but requires extensive playtesting to balance.

docs.title: Asymmetric Balance Model
docs.text:
Balance is not equality. Balance is equal opportunity for success.

flowchart LR
    FactionA["Faction A"] --> StrengthsA["Strengths A"]
    FactionA --> WeaknessesA["Weaknesses A"]
    FactionB["Faction B"] --> StrengthsB["Strengths B"]
    FactionB --> WeaknessesB["Weaknesses B"]
    StrengthsA --> Victory["Victory Potential"]
    StrengthsB --> Victory

Different toolsets require different strategies, but the potential to win is kept equal through trade-offs.

Designer takeaway

Balance does not mean sameness. It means meaningful, fair opportunity.

docs.title: The Systems Designer's Mental Model
docs.text:
Systems designers do not directly create experiences. They create rules. Rules create behaviors. Behaviors create experiences. This is one of the most important mental model shifts in game design.

flowchart TD
    Rules["Rules"] --> Behaviors["Behaviors"]
    Behaviors --> Patterns["Patterns"]
    Patterns --> Strategy["Strategy"]
    Strategy --> Meta["Meta-Game"]
    Meta --> Community["Community"]

Simple rules dictate player behaviors, which evolve into strategic patterns, forming the meta-game and fostering the community.

docs.title: From Rules To Experience
docs.text:
This is the central mental model of systems design. Designers rarely create player meaning directly. They create rule structures that produce interactions, outcomes, and eventually player meaning.

flowchart LR
    Rules["Rules"] --> Mechanics["Mechanics"]
    Mechanics --> Systems["Systems"]
    Systems --> Interactions["Interactions"]
    Interactions --> Outcomes["Player Outcomes"]
    Outcomes --> Meaning["Player Meaning"]

Meaning is derived through active participation in a game's rule structures, mechanics, systems, interactions, and player outcomes.

Designer takeaway

Systems designers do not directly design player experience. They design rules that generate player experience.

docs.title: Thinking Like a Professional Level Designer
docs.text:
One of the biggest misconceptions among new developers is that level design is about building environments.

It isn't.

Environment art builds environments.

Level design creates player experiences through space.

A professional level designer is fundamentally a behavioral architect.

Their job is not:

"Create a cool map."

Their job is:

"Create a sequence of decisions, emotions, challenges, discoveries, and behaviors that occur through interaction with space."

Senior level designers rarely think in terms of walls, doors, corridors, and props.

They think in terms of:

  • Information
  • Attention
  • Movement
  • Pressure
  • Choice
  • Risk
  • Reward
  • Pacing
  • Psychology

The map itself is merely the medium.

docs.title: The Fundamental Equation of Level Design
docs.text:
Professional level designers often operate from an implicit model:

Space+Rules+Information+Player Ability=Player Behavior

Everything in level design ultimately serves one purpose:

Shaping player behavior.

docs.title: Chapter 1: The Three Layers of a Level
docs.text: Every level exists simultaneously on three layers.

docs.title: Physical Layer
docs.text:
The literal geometry.

Examples:

  • Walls
  • Platforms
  • Buildings
  • Terrain
  • Cover objects

This is what beginners focus on.

docs.title: Functional Layer
docs.text:
The gameplay purpose.

Examples:

  • Combat space
  • Exploration space
  • Puzzle space
  • Social space

Professionals spend most of their time here.

docs.title: Experiential Layer
docs.text:
The emotional outcome.

Examples:

  • Tension
  • Curiosity
  • Relief
  • Wonder
  • Fear

Elite level designers start here.

They ask:

What should the player feel?

Then build backwards.

docs.title: Chapter 2: Flow
docs.text:
Flow is one of the most important concepts in level design.

Definition:

The smooth progression of player attention, movement, and decision-making through a space.

docs.title: Good Flow
docs.text:
Player naturally understands:

  • Where they are
  • Where they came from
  • Where they're going
  • What they're doing

Without needing explicit instruction.

docs.title: Bad Flow
docs.text:
Player experiences:

  • Confusion
  • Backtracking
  • Frustration
  • Getting lost

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
Beginners think flow means:

Moving quickly.

Not true.

A horror game may deliberately slow movement.

Flow means:

Appropriate movement for intended experience.

docs.title: Linear Flow
docs.text:
One primary route.

Examples:

  • Half-Life
  • Uncharted

Advantages:

  • Tight pacing
  • Strong narrative control

docs.title: Branching Flow
docs.text:
Multiple routes.

Examples:

  • Dishonored
  • Deus Ex

Advantages:

  • Agency
  • Replayability

docs.title: Hub-and-Spoke
docs.text:
Central hub connected to regions.

Examples:

  • Dark Souls
  • Metroid Prime

Advantages:

  • Structured exploration

docs.title: Open Flow
docs.text:
Player creates their own route.

Examples:

  • Minecraft
  • Breath of the Wild

Advantages:

  • Emergence
  • Freedom

Challenge:

  • Harder pacing control

docs.title: Chapter 3: Spatial Design
docs.text: Spatial design is the discipline of controlling experience through geometry.

docs.title: Macro Space
docs.text:
Large-scale layout.

Questions:

  • How regions connect
  • Major pathways
  • World structure

Examples:

  • Open world regions
  • Dungeon structure
  • Multiplayer map layout

docs.title: Meso Space
docs.text:
Intermediate structures.

Examples:

  • Buildings
  • Encounter zones
  • Districts

docs.title: Micro Space
docs.text:
Moment-to-moment interaction areas.

Examples:

  • Cover placements
  • Platform jumps
  • Loot locations

Many junior designers focus only on micro-space.

Senior designers work across all three scales simultaneously.

docs.title: Chapter 4: Affordances
docs.text:
One of the most important design concepts in existence.

Affordance:

What an object suggests can be done with it.

Examples:

Object Affordance Ladder Climb Door Open Lever Pull Cover wall Hide

docs.title: Why Affordances Matter
docs.text:
Players rarely read instructions.

They interpret environments.

Good affordances reduce tutorial requirements.

docs.title: False Affordance
docs.text:
Suggesting interaction that does not exist.

Example:

Detailed door that cannot open.

Players hate this.

docs.title: Hidden Affordance
docs.text:
Interaction exists but isn't communicated.

Example:

Climbable wall with no visual cues.

Players miss it.

docs.title: Signifiers
docs.text:
Often confused with affordances.

Affordance:

Possible action.

Signifier:

Information communicating affordance.

Example:

Yellow paint in modern games.

The paint isn't the affordance.

The climbable ledge is.

The paint is the signifier.

docs.title: Chapter 5: Sightlines
docs.text:
Sightlines are critical.

Definition:

What players can see from a given position.

Professional level designers obsess over sightlines.

docs.title: Long Sightlines
docs.text:
Allow:

  • Reconnaissance
  • Sniping
  • Anticipation

Common in:

  • Battlefield
  • ARMA

docs.title: Short Sightlines
docs.text:
Create:

  • Ambushes
  • Tension
  • Surprise

Common in:

  • Horror games
  • Close-quarters shooters

docs.title: Information Sightlines
docs.text:
Used for navigation.

Player sees:

  • Landmark
  • Objective
  • Exit

before reaching it.

This creates orientation.

docs.title: Chapter 6: Landmarking
docs.text:
Humans navigate using landmarks.

Players do too.

docs.title: Landmark
docs.text:
A unique environmental feature used for orientation.

Examples:

  • Castle
  • Tower
  • Statue
  • Mountain

docs.title: Strong Landmark Characteristics
docs.text: Must be:

docs.title: Visible
docs.text: Seen from multiple locations.

docs.title: Distinct
docs.text: Unique silhouette.

docs.title: Memorable
docs.text: Psychologically sticky.

docs.title: Weak Landmark
docs.text:
Everything looks similar.

Players get lost.

This is a common issue in procedural environments.

docs.title: Chapter 7: Wayfinding
docs.text:
Wayfinding is:

Helping players navigate without explicit instructions.

Elite designers guide players invisibly.

docs.title: Lighting
docs.text: Bright areas attract attention.

docs.title: Color
docs.text: Contrast attracts attention.

docs.title: Motion
docs.text: Movement naturally draws the eye.

docs.title: Framing
docs.text: Architecture can point toward objectives.

docs.title: Leading Lines
docs.text:
Roads.

Pipes.

Railings.

Hallways.

All direct vision.

docs.title: Environmental Storytelling
docs.text:
Players follow clues.

Example:

Blood trail leading to objective.

docs.title: Golden Path
docs.text:
Professional term.

Definition:

The route most players are expected to take.

Not the only route.

The preferred route.

Designers often ask:

"Can players identify the golden path?"

docs.title: Critical Path
docs.text:
Definition:

The minimum sequence required to complete objectives.

The golden path often overlaps with the critical path.

Not always.

docs.title: Chapter 8: Encounter Design
docs.text:
An encounter is:

A deliberately crafted gameplay situation.

Examples:

  • Combat
  • Puzzle
  • Stealth
  • Dialogue

docs.title: Combat Encounters
docs.text:
Professional encounter design balances:

  • Space
  • Enemies
  • Resources
  • Objectives

Not just enemy placement.

docs.title: Combat Triangle
docs.text:
Many shooters balance:

CoverMobilitySightlines

Poor balance creates stagnant gameplay.

docs.title: Arena Design
docs.text:
Arena:

Contained combat space.

Questions:

  • Where can players move?
  • Where is safety?
  • Where is danger?

docs.title: Combat Beats
docs.text:
Encounter rhythm.

Example:

Weak enemies↓Pressure increase↓Elite enemy↓Relief

Good encounters create emotional curves.

docs.title: Chapter 9: Choke Points
docs.text:
Definition:

A narrow area controlling movement.

Examples:

  • Doorways
  • Bridges
  • Tunnels

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
Control:

  • Movement
  • Combat
  • Information

docs.title: Multiplayer Usage
docs.text:
Important for:

  • Territory control
  • Defensive positions

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
Too many choke points:

Creates stagnation.

Players feel trapped.

docs.title: Chapter 10: Pacing
docs.text:
Pacing may be the most important advanced level design skill.

Definition:

The control of intensity over time.

docs.title: Intensity Curve
docs.text:
Great levels rarely maintain constant intensity.

Instead:

Calm↓Build↓Peak↓Recovery↓Build↓Peak

docs.title: Compression
docs.text: Increasing intensity.

docs.title: Release
docs.text: Reducing intensity.

docs.title: Escalation
docs.text:
Introducing new variables.

Examples:

  • New enemy type
  • Environmental hazard
  • Boss

docs.title: Chapter 11: Metrics
docs.text:
Metrics are measurements used to build levels.

Professionals rely heavily on metrics.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Character height.

Jump distance.

Sprint speed.

Weapon range.

Cover height.

Door width.

docs.title: Why Metrics Matter
docs.text:
Without metrics:

Levels become inconsistent.

Consistency creates readability.

docs.title: Grayboxing / Blockouts
docs.text:
Professional workflow:

Gameplay↓Testing↓Iteration↓Art Pass

NOT:

Art↓Gameplay

docs.title: Graybox
docs.text:
Simple geometry used to test design.

Examples:

  • Cubes
  • Planes
  • Basic shapes

Purpose:

Validate gameplay.

docs.title: Chapter 12: Player Funnels
docs.text:
A funnel narrows player movement.

Used for:

  • Guidance
  • Pacing
  • Dramatic reveals

docs.title: Hard Funnel
docs.text:
Physical restriction.

Examples:

  • Corridor
  • Locked gate

docs.title: Soft Funnel
docs.text:
Psychological guidance.

Examples:

  • Lighting
  • Visual interest
  • Objectives

Senior designers prefer soft funnels whenever possible.

docs.title: Chapter 13: Environmental Storytelling
docs.text:
One of the most misunderstood concepts.

Definition:

Narrative communicated through environment rather than exposition.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Destroyed laboratory.

Broken containment chamber.

Blood trail.

No dialogue needed.

Player constructs story.

docs.title: Passive Storytelling
docs.text:
Information exists.

Player may ignore it.

docs.title: Active Storytelling
docs.text:
Player interaction reveals story.

Examples:

  • Notes
  • Audio logs
  • Interactive objects

docs.title: FPS Design
docs.text:
Focuses on:

  • Sightlines
  • Cover
  • Combat rhythm

Questions:

Can players read combat space?

docs.title: Third-Person Action
docs.text:
Focuses on:

  • Camera management
  • Traversal readability
  • Encounter staging

docs.title: RPG Design
docs.text:
Focuses on:

  • Exploration
  • Quest routing
  • Environmental storytelling

docs.title: Strategy Games
docs.text:
Focuses on:

  • Resource geography
  • Territorial control
  • Information asymmetry

docs.title: Simulation Games
docs.text:
Focuses on:

  • System interaction
  • Emergent behavior
  • Efficiency

docs.title: Horror Games
docs.text:
Focuses on:

  • Uncertainty
  • Claustrophobia
  • Controlled information

docs.title: Multiplayer Maps
docs.text:
Focuses on:

  • Fairness
  • Symmetry
  • Counterplay
  • Predictability

docs.title: Chapter 15: Senior-Level Vocabulary
docs.text: These are phrases you will hear in professional reviews.

docs.title: Readability
docs.text: Can players understand what's happening?

docs.title: Visual Noise
docs.text: Information that competes for attention.

docs.title: Cognitive Load
docs.text: Mental effort required.

docs.title: Decision Density
docs.text: Number of meaningful decisions per minute.

docs.title: Dead Space
docs.text: Area lacking meaningful interaction.

docs.title: Kill Zone
docs.text: Area exposed to danger.

docs.title: Safe Space
docs.text: Area offering recovery.

docs.title: Combat Readability
docs.text: Can players interpret threats?

docs.title: Traversal Language
docs.text: Consistent communication of movement opportunities.

docs.title: Environmental Language
docs.text:
Repeated visual rules players learn.

Example:

Yellow = climbable.

Red barrel = explosive.

docs.title: The Master Mental Model of Level Design
docs.text:
Beginners think:

"How do I build this environment?"

Intermediate designers think:

"How do I create this gameplay?"

Senior designers think:

"How do I shape player behavior?"

Expert designers think:

"How do I shape player psychology through space, information, and choice?"

That final transition is what separates a map creator from a true level designer.

docs.title: Professional Exercise
docs.text:
When playing any game, stop every 5 minutes and ask:

  1. What behavior is this space encouraging?
  2. What behavior is it discouraging?
  3. What information is being revealed?
  4. What information is being hidden?
  5. Where is the player's attention being directed?
  6. What emotional state is the designer attempting to create?
  7. What level-design techniques are being used to achieve it?

If you can consistently answer those questions, you will begin to see levels the way professional designers do.

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What “Art” Means in Game Development
docs.text:
In game development, “art” is not just visual quality.

Professionally, art is defined as:

A communication system that encodes gameplay meaning, emotional tone, identity, and functional clarity into visual form.

This is critical:

Senior artists are not primarily thinking about “making things look good.”

They are thinking about:

  • Readability
  • Function
  • Identity
  • Emotional tone
  • Technical constraints
  • Production scalability
  • Cross-discipline communication

A common AAA phrase:

“Art serves gameplay, gameplay serves clarity.”

docs.title: Chapter 1: The Art Pipeline (End-to-End Production Language)
docs.text: Every visual asset goes through a structured pipeline.

docs.title: 1. Pre-Production (Visual Development)
docs.text:
This phase defines:

  • Style direction
  • Visual language
  • Mood
  • References
  • Constraints

docs.title: Visual Development (VisDev)
docs.text:
The exploration phase where the game’s visual identity is defined.

Includes:

  • Concept sketches
  • Mood exploration
  • Style tests

docs.title: Art Bible
docs.text:
A foundational document defining:

  • Style rules
  • Color palette
  • Shape language
  • Lighting rules
  • Reference benchmarks

Used to enforce consistency across teams.

docs.title: Mood Board
docs.text:
A curated collection of reference imagery that defines:

  • Tone
  • Emotion
  • Style direction

Not random images — but intentional visual alignment.

docs.title: Style Guide
docs.text:
A stricter, production-facing version of the Art Bible.

Used to prevent:

  • Visual inconsistency
  • Asset drift across teams

docs.title: Chapter 2: Concept Art Terminology
docs.text:
Concept art is not final art.

It is:

Visual problem-solving.

docs.title: Thumbnailing
docs.text:
Rapid small sketches used to explore composition and ideas.

Purpose:

  • Speed over detail
  • Explore many ideas quickly

Professionals may produce dozens or hundreds.

docs.title: Iteration
docs.text:
Repeated refinement cycles.

Example:

Thumbnail → Sketch → Refined Concept → Final Concept

docs.title: Keyshot / Hero Shot
docs.text:
A fully realized concept image showing:

  • Lighting
  • Composition
  • Mood
  • Final intended look

Used for marketing and approval.

docs.title: Silhouette Exploration
docs.text:
Testing readability of forms in black shape only.

Why it matters:

If a character is recognizable in silhouette, it reads well in gameplay.

docs.title: Shape Language
docs.text:
The emotional meaning of shapes.

Shape Meaning Circles Friendly, soft Squares Stable, strong Triangles Dangerous, aggressive

Used heavily in character and environment design.

docs.title: Visual Hierarchy
docs.text:
Controlling what the player sees first, second, third.

Achieved via:

  • Contrast
  • Color
  • Scale
  • Lighting

docs.title: Modular Design
docs.text:
Creating reusable asset pieces.

Example:

  • Walls
  • Floors
  • Corners
  • Pillars

Allows efficient level building.

docs.title: Kitbashing
docs.text:
Assembling new assets from existing pieces.

Used heavily in:

  • Sci-fi environments
  • Industrial structures

docs.title: Dressing
docs.text:
Placing props into a level after blockout.

Not random decoration:

It supports:

  • Storytelling
  • Navigation
  • Atmosphere

docs.title: Set Dressing Language
docs.text:
Professional term for environmental storytelling through props.

Example:

  • Broken chairs → conflict
  • Papers everywhere → panic
  • Barricades → defense

docs.title: Texel Density
docs.text:
Consistency of texture resolution across assets.

Why it matters:

Prevents visual mismatch.

docs.title: UV Mapping
docs.text: Flattening 3D surfaces into 2D space for texturing.

docs.title: Baking
docs.text:
Transferring high-detail information to low-poly models.

Includes:

  • Normal maps
  • Ambient occlusion
  • Curvature maps

docs.title: PBR (Physically Based Rendering)
docs.text:
Modern rendering system based on real-world light behavior.

Key components:

  • Albedo
  • Roughness
  • Metallic
  • Normal maps

docs.title: Material System
docs.text:
Defines how surfaces respond to light.

Examples:

  • Metal
  • Wood
  • Fabric
  • Skin

docs.title: Topology
docs.text:
The structure of polygon flow.

Good topology:

  • Supports animation
  • Optimized for performance

Bad topology:

  • Causes deformation artifacts
  • Wastes polygons

docs.title: Retopology
docs.text:
Rebuilding high-poly models into optimized mesh structures.

Used after sculpting.

docs.title: Hard Surface Modeling
docs.text:
Modeling mechanical objects:

  • Weapons
  • Vehicles
  • Buildings

Focus:

Precision and clean edges.

docs.title: Organic Modeling
docs.text:
Modeling natural forms:

  • Characters
  • Creatures
  • Cloth

Focus:

Flow and deformation.

docs.title: Sculpting
docs.text:
High-detail modeling using digital clay tools.

Used for:

  • Fine detail
  • Surface imperfections
  • High-poly sources for baking

docs.title: Keyframes
docs.text:
Primary poses defining motion.

Everything between is interpolated.

docs.title: In-Betweens
docs.text: Intermediate frames between key poses.

docs.title: Rigging
docs.text:
Creating a skeletal structure for animation.

Includes:

  • Bones
  • Constraints
  • Controls

docs.title: IK (Inverse Kinematics)
docs.text:
End-effector-driven motion.

Example:

Foot stays planted while body moves.

docs.title: FK (Forward Kinematics)
docs.text:
Chain-driven motion.

Example:

Rotating shoulder affects arm and hand.

docs.title: Blend Trees
docs.text:
Systems that blend multiple animations.

Example:

Walking → running → sprinting smoothly.

docs.title: State Machines
docs.text:
Animation logic systems controlling transitions.

Example:

Idle → Walk → Run → Jump

docs.title: Root Motion
docs.text:
Animation drives character movement directly.

Alternative:

Movement driven by code.

docs.title: Motion Matching
docs.text:
Advanced system selecting best animation frame-by-frame.

Used in modern AAA locomotion systems.

docs.title: Chapter 6: Technical Art (Critical Bridge Discipline)
docs.text:
Technical art is where art meets engineering.

A technical artist is often described as:

The translator between artists and engineers.

docs.title: Shader Development
docs.text:
Writing programs that define surface rendering behavior.

Used for:

  • Water
  • Skin
  • Glass
  • Stylized effects

docs.title: Material Editor
docs.text: Node-based system for building shaders visually.

docs.title: VFX Systems
docs.text:
Visual effects such as:

  • Explosions
  • Smoke
  • Magic spells
  • Weather

docs.title: Particle Systems
docs.text: Simulated collections of small visual elements.

docs.title: GPU Optimization
docs.text:
Ensuring effects run efficiently on hardware.

Includes:

  • Reducing overdraw
  • Limiting shader complexity

docs.title: Draw Calls
docs.text:
Instructions sent from CPU to GPU to render objects.

Fewer draw calls = better performance.

docs.title: Overdraw
docs.text:
Pixels rendered multiple times unnecessarily.

Common in:

  • VFX
  • Transparency-heavy scenes

docs.title: Global Illumination (GI)
docs.text:
Simulation of indirect light bounce.

Creates realism and depth.

docs.title: Light Baking
docs.text:
Pre-calculating lighting into textures.

Used for performance optimization.

docs.title: Dynamic Lighting
docs.text:
Real-time lighting calculations.

More expensive but flexible.

docs.title: Ambient Occlusion (AO)
docs.text:
Simulated shadowing in creases and corners.

Adds depth perception.

docs.title: Post Processing
docs.text:
Effects applied after rendering:

  • Bloom
  • Color grading
  • Motion blur

docs.title: Color Grading
docs.text:
Adjusting final image tone.

Used heavily for mood.

docs.title: How Artists Speak to Designers
docs.text:
Instead of:

“Make it look cooler”

They say:

  • “Improve readability of this path”
  • “Increase visual hierarchy here”
  • “Clarify player intention at this junction”

docs.title: How Designers Speak to Artists
docs.text:
Instead of:

“Make this area fun”

They say:

  • “This space should communicate danger and reward simultaneously”
  • “We need a strong focal point to guide navigation”

docs.title: How Technical Artists Translate
docs.text:
They convert:

Art intent → engine constraints

Example:

“We want volumetric fog”

becomes:

“We can approximate this with layered particles + depth-based falloff”

docs.title: Art Director Role
docs.text:
The art director ensures:

  • Consistency
  • Style cohesion
  • Emotional alignment
  • Brand identity

They do not typically create assets.

They define vision.

docs.title: HUD (Heads-Up Display)
docs.text:
On-screen gameplay information.

Examples:

  • Health bars
  • Ammo counters
  • Minimap

docs.title: Diegetic UI
docs.text:
UI existing inside the game world.

Example:

Dead Space health bar on character suit.

docs.title: Non-Diegetic UI
docs.text: Traditional overlay UI.

docs.title: Visual Noise
docs.text: UI clutter reducing readability.

docs.title: Information Hierarchy
docs.text: What the player notices first.

docs.title: Affordance in UI
docs.text: Buttons must look clickable.

docs.title: Chapter 10: Animation Language in Production Context
Animation Polish
docs.text:
Improving:

  • Timing
  • Weight
  • Fluidity

docs.title: Anticipation
docs.text:
Movement before action.

Example:

Character pulls back before punching.

docs.title: Follow-through
docs.text: Continuation after motion ends.

docs.title: Ease In / Ease Out
docs.text: Acceleration curves of motion.

docs.title: Chapter 11: Industry Reality of Art Production
Concept vs Production Art
docs.text: Concept Production Exploratory Final Flexible Locked Fast Optimized

docs.title: Asset Lock
docs.text: Point where changes become expensive.

docs.title: Revisions
docs.text:
Iteration cycles after feedback.

Common in:

  • Art reviews
  • Milestone reviews

docs.title: Art Passes
docs.text:
Layered refinement stages:

  1. Blockout pass
  2. Material pass
  3. Lighting pass
  4. Polish pass

docs.title: Chapter 12: Senior-Level Mental Model of Art in Games
docs.text:
Beginners think:

“How do I make this look good?”

Intermediate artists think:

“How do I make this consistent with style?”

Senior professionals think:

“How does this visual element communicate gameplay information, emotional tone, and system clarity under production constraints?”

Expert artists think:

“How does every visual decision support player understanding without explanation?”

docs.title: Key Insight (Critical)
docs.text:
In AAA development:

Art is not decoration. It is information design in visual form.

docs.title: Transition to Next Discipline
docs.text:
We have now covered:

  • Game development foundations
  • Game design language
  • Level design language
  • Art & visual development language

Next we move into a discipline that connects everything:

docs.title: 👉 Technical Art (deep engineering bridge layer) OR Programming & Game Engineering
docs.text:
This is where:

  • systems become code
  • visuals become runtime behavior
  • gameplay becomes architecture

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What “Programming” Means in Games
docs.text:
In game development, programming is not just “writing code.”

Professionally, it is:

The construction of real-time simulation systems that must remain stable, performant, and responsive under unpredictable user behavior and hardware constraints.

Game programming is fundamentally different from most software engineering because:

  • Everything is real-time
  • Everything is interactive
  • Everything is performance-sensitive
  • Everything is failure-visible (instantly to players)

A common AAA framing:

“If backend software is about correctness, game code is about correctness under pressure.”

docs.title: Chapter 1: The Game Loop — The Heart of Everything
docs.text: At the core of every game is the game loop.

docs.title: Game Loop
docs.text:
A repeating cycle that updates simulation every frame.

Input → Update → Simulation → Render → Repeat

This loop runs ~30–240 times per second depending on platform.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text:
Everything in a game exists inside this loop:

  • Player movement
  • AI behavior
  • Physics
  • Animation
  • UI updates

If it is not in the loop, it is not “alive.”

docs.title: Frame
docs.text:
A single iteration of the game loop.

Professionals often say:

  • “Frame spike”
  • “Frame budget”
  • “Frame time”

docs.title: Frame Time
docs.text:
How long one frame takes to compute.

Example:

  • 16.6 ms → 60 FPS
  • 33 ms → 30 FPS

docs.title: Tick
docs.text:
A simulation update step.

Sometimes decoupled from rendering.

docs.title: Delta Time
docs.text:
Time between frames.

Used to make movement consistent across frame rates.

docs.title: Chapter 2: Architecture Thinking (How Professionals Structure Code)
docs.text:
Game code is not written as a flat script.

It is structured into systems.

docs.title: System Architecture
docs.text:
A system is:

A self-contained module that manages a domain of gameplay or engine behavior.

Examples:

  • Physics system
  • Animation system
  • Input system
  • AI system

docs.title: ECS (Entity Component System)
docs.text: A modern architecture pattern.

docs.title: Entity
docs.text:
A unique ID.

No behavior.

docs.title: Component
docs.text:
Data only.

Example:

  • Position
  • Health
  • Velocity

docs.title: System
docs.text:
Logic that operates on components.

Example:

  • Movement system updates positions

docs.title: Why ECS exists
docs.text:
Traditional OOP struggles with:

  • Performance
  • Cache efficiency
  • Large-scale simulation

ECS solves this by prioritizing:

Data over hierarchy

docs.title: OOP (Object-Oriented Programming)
docs.text:
Classic model:

  • Classes
  • Inheritance
  • Encapsulation

Used heavily in older engines and still common in gameplay code.

docs.title: Data-Oriented Design (DOD)
docs.text:
Design philosophy:

Optimize for how data is accessed, not how objects are modeled.

Used in AAA engines for performance-critical systems.

docs.title: Composition over Inheritance
docs.text:
Modern principle:

Build behavior from modular pieces instead of deep class trees.

docs.title: State Machines
docs.text:
A system where behavior is defined by discrete states.

Example:

Idle → Walk → Run → Jump → Fall → Land

Used in:

  • Characters
  • AI
  • UI flow
  • Animation

docs.title: Finite State Machine (FSM)
docs.text: A state machine with limited defined transitions.

docs.title: Event System
docs.text:
A system where actions trigger notifications.

Example:

  • Player dies → event fires → UI updates → sound plays

Decouples systems.

docs.title: Observer Pattern
docs.text: Design pattern used for event systems.

docs.title: Input System
docs.text:
Handles player control devices:

  • Keyboard
  • Mouse
  • Gamepad

Modern systems separate:

  • Raw input
  • Action mapping
  • Context-sensitive input

docs.title: Physics Engine
docs.text:
Simulates:

  • Gravity
  • Collisions
  • Forces
  • Momentum

docs.title: Collision Detection
docs.text:
Determining when objects intersect.

Types:

  • AABB (Axis-Aligned Bounding Box)
  • Sphere collision
  • Mesh collision

docs.title: Rigidbody
docs.text: Object affected by physics simulation.

docs.title: Kinematic Object
docs.text: Object moved manually, not by physics forces.

docs.title: Raycasting
docs.text:
Casting an invisible line to detect hits.

Used for:

  • Shooting
  • AI vision
  • Interaction detection

docs.title: Hitscan
docs.text:
Instant hit detection.

Example: laser gun.

docs.title: Projectile
docs.text:
Physical object travels through space.

Example: bullet, arrow.

docs.title: Behavior Tree
docs.text:
Hierarchical AI decision system.

Structure:

  • Selector nodes
  • Sequence nodes
  • Leaf actions

docs.title: Utility AI
docs.text:
AI chooses actions based on weighted scoring.

More dynamic than behavior trees.

docs.title: Pathfinding
docs.text:
Finding optimal route through space.

Most common algorithm:

docs.title: NavMesh (Navigation Mesh)
docs.text:
Precomputed walkable surfaces.

Used by AI agents.

docs.title: Steering Behaviors
docs.text:
Local movement rules:

  • Seek
  • Flee
  • Avoid
  • Wander

docs.title: Profiling
docs.text:
Measuring performance bottlenecks.

Tools show:

  • CPU usage
  • GPU usage
  • Memory usage

docs.title: Bottleneck
docs.text: The slowest part of a system limiting performance.

docs.title: CPU Bound
docs.text: Limited by processing power.

docs.title: GPU Bound
docs.text: Limited by rendering complexity.

docs.title: Draw Calls
docs.text:
Instructions sent to GPU to render objects.

Too many = performance issues.

docs.title: Batching
docs.text: Combining multiple objects into fewer draw calls.

docs.title: Occlusion Culling
docs.text: Not rendering objects hidden behind others.

docs.title: LOD (Level of Detail)
docs.text:
Using simpler models at distance.

Example:

  • High-poly close
  • Low-poly far

docs.title: Memory Management
docs.text:
Handling RAM usage efficiently.

Includes:

  • Allocation
  • Garbage collection
  • Pooling

docs.title: Object Pooling
docs.text:
Reusing objects instead of creating/destroying them.

Used for:

  • Bullets
  • Particles
  • Enemies

docs.title: Client-Server Architecture
docs.text: Most common multiplayer model.

docs.title: Client
docs.text: Player machine.

docs.title: Server
docs.text: Authoritative game state.

docs.title: Authority
docs.text:
Who decides truth.

In modern games:

Server is usually authoritative.

docs.title: Peer-to-Peer (P2P)
docs.text:
Players connect directly.

Less common due to cheating and instability.

docs.title: Latency
docs.text: Delay between action and response.

docs.title: Lag Compensation
docs.text:
Techniques to hide latency.

Examples:

  • Hit rewinding
  • Client prediction

docs.title: Client Prediction
docs.text: Client assumes result before server confirms.

docs.title: Server Reconciliation
docs.text: Correcting client when server disagrees.

docs.title: Replication
docs.text: Synchronizing state across network.

docs.title: Tick Rate
docs.text:
How often server updates.

Higher tick rate = smoother gameplay but higher cost.

docs.title: Rollback Netcode
docs.text:
Used in fighting games.

System:

  • Simulate future guesses
  • Roll back if incorrect
  • Recompute state

docs.title: Game Engine
docs.text:
Software framework providing:

  • Rendering
  • Physics
  • Audio
  • Input
  • Tools

Examples:

  • Unreal Engine
  • Unity

docs.title: Middleware
docs.text:
External tools integrated into engine.

Examples:

  • Physics engines
  • Audio systems

docs.title: Scripting Layer
docs.text:
High-level gameplay logic layer.

Often uses:

  • Lua
  • C#
  • Blueprints

docs.title: Rendering Pipeline
docs.text:
Sequence transforming 3D data into final image.

Stages:

  • Geometry processing
  • Lighting
  • Rasterization
  • Post-processing

docs.title: Bug
docs.text: Unexpected behavior.

docs.title: Repro Steps
docs.text: Steps to consistently trigger a bug.

docs.title: Edge Case
docs.text: Rare or extreme scenario.

docs.title: Crash
docs.text: Program termination due to critical failure.

docs.title: Memory Leak
docs.text: Memory not properly released.

docs.title: Null Reference
docs.text: Missing object reference.

docs.title: Race Condition
docs.text: Timing-dependent bug in concurrent systems.

docs.title: Chapter 10: Build Systems & Production Code Language
Build
docs.text: Compiled version of the game.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text: Automated system turning code into playable builds.

docs.title: CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Deployment)
docs.text: Automated testing and build generation.

docs.title: Version Control
docs.text:
Tracking code changes.

Common system:

  • Git

docs.title: Merge Conflict
docs.text: When two changes overlap.

docs.title: Branching
docs.text: Parallel development streams.

docs.title: Chapter 11: Gameplay Programming vs Engine Programming
Gameplay Programmer
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Player behavior
  • Mechanics
  • Systems interaction

docs.title: Engine Programmer
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Rendering
  • Physics
  • Performance
  • Core architecture

docs.title: Tools Programmer
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Editor tools
  • Workflow optimization
  • Production efficiency

docs.title: Technical Director (TD)
docs.text:
Focus:

High-level technical vision and system architecture.

docs.title: Systems Thinking
docs.text: Understanding how components interact as a whole.

docs.title: Determinism
docs.text:
Same input → same output.

Important for:

  • Multiplayer
  • Replays

docs.title: Emergent Complexity
docs.text: Simple rules producing complex behavior.

docs.title: Abstraction Layers
docs.text:
Separating complexity into levels.

Example:

  • Input layer
  • Gameplay layer
  • Engine layer

docs.title: Performance Budgeting
docs.text:
Allocating CPU/GPU cost across systems.

Example:

  • 5 ms AI
  • 6 ms rendering
  • 2 ms physics

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
Beginners think:

“How do I make this feature work?”

Intermediate programmers think:

“How do I structure this system cleanly?”

Senior engineers think:

“How do I design systems that remain stable, scalable, and debuggable under real-time constraints?”

Expert engineers think:

“How do I model simulation truth across CPU, GPU, network, and player perception simultaneously?”

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What “Audio” Means in Games
docs.text:
In games, audio is not decoration.

It is:

A real-time feedback, emotion, and information system that operates in parallel with visuals and gameplay mechanics.

In professional terms:

  • Visuals show what is happening
  • Gameplay defines what is happening
  • Audio tells you how it feels and often what to pay attention to first

A common AAA insight:

“Players forgive visual confusion before they forgive audio confusion.”

Because sound is often the fastest communication channel in games.

docs.title: Chapter 1: The Three Functions of Game Audio
docs.text: Every sound in a game typically serves at least one of three roles:

docs.title: 1. Informational Audio
docs.text:
Audio that communicates gameplay state.

Examples:

  • Low health warning
  • Enemy nearby
  • Objective complete
  • Reload click

This is often called:

Feedback audio

docs.title: 2. Emotional Audio
docs.text:
Audio that shapes mood.

Examples:

  • Ambient wind in horror games
  • Orchestral tension buildup
  • Peaceful environmental music

This is often called:

Atmospheric audio

docs.title: 3. Mechanical Audio
docs.text:
Audio tied directly to player actions.

Examples:

  • Footsteps
  • Gunshots
  • Jump sounds
  • UI clicks

This is often called:

Diegetic action feedback

docs.title: Sound Design
docs.text:
The creation and shaping of audio assets for gameplay and emotion.

Not just recording sounds, but:

  • Layering
  • Processing
  • Timing
  • Contextual triggering

docs.title: Foley
docs.text:
Re-recording real-world actions in a studio.

Examples:

  • Footsteps
  • Cloth movement
  • Object handling

Named after early film sound pioneer Jack Foley.

docs.title: SFX (Sound Effects)
docs.text:
General term for non-music audio assets.

Includes:

  • Weapons
  • UI sounds
  • Impacts
  • Environmental sounds

docs.title: One-Shot Sound
docs.text:
A sound that plays fully once.

Example:

  • Gunshot
  • Door slam

docs.title: Loop
docs.text:
A sound that repeats continuously.

Example:

  • Engine hum
  • Wind ambience

docs.title: Layering
docs.text:
Combining multiple sounds into one composite sound.

Example:

A gunshot may include:

  • Trigger click
  • Explosion
  • Mechanical echo
  • Sub-bass impact

docs.title: Transient
docs.text:
The initial attack portion of a sound.

Critical for:

Perceived impact and responsiveness

docs.title: Tail
docs.text:
The decay portion of a sound.

Example:

  • Reverb
  • Echo
  • Fade-out

docs.title: Adaptive Audio
docs.text:
Audio that changes dynamically based on game state.

Example:

  • Combat music intensifies when enemies appear
  • Calm music returns when safe

docs.title: Interactive Music
docs.text:
Music that responds to gameplay input or events.

Example:

  • Adding percussion during combat
  • Removing layers during stealth

docs.title: Stingers
docs.text:
Short musical cues triggered by events.

Example:

  • Enemy spotted
  • Boss phase change

docs.title: Music Layering System
docs.text:
Music broken into layers:

  • Base layer (ambient)
  • Tension layer
  • Combat layer
  • Boss layer

System dynamically mixes them.

docs.title: Vertical Re-Orchestration
docs.text:
Changing arrangement instead of just intensity.

Example:

Same melody, different instrumentation during combat.

docs.title: Horizontal Re-Sequencing
docs.text:
Switching between different musical sections.

Example:

Exploration → combat → victory themes.

docs.title: Spatial Audio
docs.text:
Audio positioned in 3D space relative to player.

Enables:

  • Directionality
  • Distance perception
  • Environmental realism

docs.title: 3D Audio Source
docs.text:
Sound emitted from a point in world space.

Example:

Enemy footsteps behind player.

docs.title: Attenuation
docs.text: Reduction of volume over distance.

docs.title: Occlusion
docs.text:
Sound being blocked by objects.

Example:

Sound muffled behind a wall.

docs.title: Reverb
docs.text:
Echo effect based on environment.

Examples:

  • Cave → long reverb
  • Room → short reverb
  • Outdoors → minimal reverb

docs.title: HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function)
docs.text:
Mathematical model simulating how human ears perceive direction.

Used in:

  • VR audio
  • 3D headphone sound

docs.title: Audio Middleware
docs.text:
External systems that manage game audio logic.

Used because engines alone are often too limited.

Common tools:

  • FMOD
  • Wwise

docs.title: Event-Based Audio System
docs.text:
Audio triggered by events rather than hardcoded calls.

Example:

Player jumps → event triggers → sound system plays jump sound

This decouples audio from gameplay code.

docs.title: Audio Events
docs.text:
Named triggers for sound playback.

Example:

  • Play_Explosion_Large
  • Play_UI_Click
  • Play_Enemy_Spawn

docs.title: Parameter-Based Audio
docs.text:
Audio changes based on variables.

Examples:

  • Speed affects engine pitch
  • Health affects heartbeat intensity

docs.title: RTPC (Real-Time Parameter Control)
docs.text:
Used in Wwise.

Allows continuous audio modulation.

Example:

  • Danger level → music intensity

docs.title: Mixing
docs.text:
Balancing all audio elements together.

Includes:

  • Volume balance
  • Frequency separation
  • Spatial placement

docs.title: Mastering
docs.text:
Final audio processing step.

Ensures:

  • Consistency
  • Loudness standards
  • Platform compliance

docs.title: Dynamic Range
docs.text: Difference between quiet and loud sounds.

docs.title: Compression (Audio)
docs.text:
Reducing dynamic range.

Used to:

  • Make sounds more consistent
  • Improve clarity in gameplay

docs.title: EQ (Equalization)
docs.text:
Adjusting frequency balance.

Example:

  • Removing bass muddiness
  • Enhancing clarity

docs.title: Loudness Normalization
docs.text:
Ensuring consistent perceived volume.

Important for:

  • Consoles
  • Streaming platforms

docs.title: Audio Feedback Loop
docs.text:
Audio confirms player actions.

Example:

  • Shooting → gun sound → hit marker sound

This reinforces:

Agency and responsiveness

docs.title: Auditory Masking
docs.text:
When one sound hides another.

Example:

Explosion masking footsteps.

docs.title: Audio Hierarchy
docs.text:
Prioritizing important sounds.

Example:

  • Enemy attack > ambient wind > UI click

docs.title: Cognitive Load Reduction
docs.text:
Good audio reduces need for visual attention.

Example:

You hear enemy behind you before seeing them.

docs.title: Pavlovian Audio Design
docs.text:
Conditioned responses through repetition.

Example:

  • Loot sound = reward dopamine trigger

Used heavily in:

  • Loot games
  • Live service games

docs.title: FPS Audio
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Positional accuracy
  • Weapon clarity
  • Tactical feedback

Key terms:

  • Footstep occlusion
  • Weapon tail clarity
  • Distance attenuation

docs.title: Horror Audio
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Ambiguity
  • Unease
  • Absence of sound

Key concepts:

  • Silence design
  • Sub-bass tension
  • Unresolved audio cues

docs.title: RPG Audio
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Emotional atmosphere
  • World immersion
  • Long-form ambience

docs.title: Multiplayer Audio
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Competitive clarity
  • Fast recognition
  • Priority sounds

docs.title: Mobile Audio
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Compression
  • Clarity on speakers
  • Battery-friendly processing

docs.title: Audio Engine
docs.text: System that processes and plays sound.

docs.title: Voice Limiting
docs.text:
Limiting number of simultaneous sounds.

Prevents audio overload.

docs.title: Audio Pooling
docs.text: Reusing audio instances for performance.

docs.title: Streaming Audio
docs.text:
Loading long sounds dynamically.

Example:

Music tracks, cutscenes.

docs.title: Sample Rate
docs.text:
How many audio samples per second.

Higher = better quality, more cost.

docs.title: Bit Depth
docs.text:
Resolution of audio amplitude.

Affects dynamic range quality.

docs.title: Audio Thread
docs.text:
Separate processing thread for sound.

Prevents blocking gameplay loop.

docs.title: Latency
docs.text:
Delay between trigger and playback.

Critical for responsiveness.

docs.title: Audio Tick
docs.text: Regular update cycle for audio system.

docs.title: Event Queue
docs.text: System storing pending audio events.

docs.title: Designers → Audio Team
docs.text:
Instead of:

“Make it sound cool”

They say:

  • “This attack needs stronger telegraphing”
  • “We need clearer hit confirmation”
  • “This area should feel unsafe audibly”

docs.title: Programmers → Audio Team
docs.text:
Instead of:

“Play sound here”

They say:

  • “Trigger event on state change”
  • “Expose parameter for intensity scaling”

docs.title: Artists → Audio Team
docs.text:
Instead of:

“This should feel scary”

They say:

  • “We want low-frequency rumble here”
  • “Space feels enclosed visually, audio should reinforce that”

docs.title: Audio Director Role
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • Overall sound identity
  • Consistency across systems
  • Emotional alignment
  • Mixing standards

Not just sound creation — sound strategy.

docs.title: Chapter 12: Senior-Level Audio Mental Model
docs.text:
Beginners think:

“What sound should this object make?”

Intermediate developers think:

“How do I make this sound realistic?”

Senior audio designers think:

“What information, emotion, and feedback should this sound communicate within the player’s cognitive load?”

Expert audio designers think:

“How does sound shape player attention, reaction timing, emotional state, and perceived responsiveness in real time?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In high-level game development:

Audio is not an effect. It is a communication channel faster than vision.

docs.title: Chapter 7 — UX & UI (User Experience & User Interface Terminology)
The Core Mental Shift: What UX/UI Means in Games
docs.text:
In games, UX/UI is not “menus and HUDs.”

Professionally, it is:

The system that translates complex simulation state into human-readable, actionable information under real-time pressure.

A strong AAA principle:

“If the player is confused, the UI has failed — even if the system is working perfectly.”

UX/UI is fundamentally about:

  • Clarity
  • Decision support
  • Cognitive load management
  • Attention guidance
  • Interaction speed

docs.title: Chapter 1: UX vs UI (Critical Distinction)
docs.text: These terms are often confused.

docs.title: UI (User Interface)
docs.text:
The visual and interactive layer.

Examples:

  • HUD
  • Menus
  • Buttons
  • Icons
  • Maps

UI is what the player sees and interacts with.

docs.title: UX (User Experience)
docs.text:
The total experience of interacting with the game systems.

Includes:

  • Navigation flow
  • Menu friction
  • Clarity of systems
  • Tutorial effectiveness
  • Feedback timing

UX exists even without visible UI.

Example:

  • Movement feel = UX
  • Inventory frustration = UX
  • Confusing quest flow = UX

docs.title: Information Architecture
docs.text:
The structural organization of information.

In games:

How systems are grouped, labeled, and accessed.

Examples:

  • Inventory → Equipment → Crafting
  • Main menu → Settings → Audio → Controls

docs.title: Why IA matters
docs.text:
Poor IA causes:

  • Player frustration
  • Feature invisibility
  • Cognitive overload

Good IA makes complexity feel simple.

docs.title: Cognitive Load
docs.text: The amount of mental effort required to understand a system.

docs.title: Intrinsic Load
docs.text:
The natural complexity of the system.

Example:

Learning chess rules.

docs.title: Extraneous Load
docs.text:
Unnecessary confusion caused by poor design.

Example:

Bad UI layout.

docs.title: Germane Load
docs.text:
Useful mental effort that builds mastery.

Example:

Learning combat systems over time.

docs.title: UX Goal:
docs.text: Minimize extraneous load while preserving meaningful intrinsic load.

docs.title: Interaction Design
docs.text:
How players interact with systems.

Focus:

  • Inputs
  • Responses
  • Feedback loops

docs.title: Input Mapping
docs.text:
Mapping controls to actions.

Example:

  • A = Jump
  • RT = Shoot

docs.title: Responsiveness
docs.text:
How quickly the game reacts to input.

Critical concept:

“Input delay breaks immersion more than visual quality issues.”

docs.title: Feedback Timing
docs.text:
Time between action and response.

Examples:

  • Hit confirmation sound
  • Animation response
  • UI flash

docs.title: HUD
docs.text:
On-screen overlay showing game state.

Examples:

  • Health bar
  • Ammo count
  • Objective tracker

docs.title: Diegetic UI
docs.text:
UI existing within the game world.

Example:

  • Health shown on character suit (Dead Space style)

docs.title: Non-Diegetic UI
docs.text:
Traditional overlay UI.

Example:

  • Floating minimap
  • Ammo counters

docs.title: Meta UI
docs.text:
Out-of-world systems.

Example:

  • Pause menus
  • Settings screens

docs.title: Visual Hierarchy
docs.text:
The order in which the eye processes information.

Controlled through:

  • Size
  • Contrast
  • Color
  • Position
  • Motion

docs.title: Primary Information
docs.text:
What must be seen immediately.

Examples:

  • Low health warning
  • Enemy indicators

docs.title: Secondary Information
docs.text:
Supporting data.

Examples:

  • Ammo
  • Mini-map details

docs.title: Tertiary Information
docs.text:
Optional or contextual data.

Examples:

  • Flavor text
  • Lore descriptions

docs.title: UI Affordance
docs.text:
How an element suggests interaction.

Examples:

  • Buttons look pressable
  • Sliders look draggable
  • Icons suggest function

docs.title: Signifiers
docs.text:
Visual cues that indicate interaction.

Example:

  • Highlight on hover
  • Glow on selection

docs.title: False Affordance
docs.text:
UI element looks interactive but is not.

This is a major UX failure.

docs.title: Usability Testing
docs.text:
Observing real players interacting with the game.

Goal:

Identify confusion without developer bias.

docs.title: Heatmaps
docs.text:
Visual data showing where players look or click.

Used in:

  • UI optimization
  • Tutorial design

docs.title: Playtesting
docs.text:
