Structured gameplay observation.

Key questions:

  • Where do players get stuck?
  • What do they misunderstand?
  • What do they ignore?

docs.title: Telemetry
docs.text:
Quantitative behavioral data.

Examples:

  • Time spent in menus
  • Death locations
  • Drop-off points

docs.title: Accessibility
docs.text:
Designing systems usable by players with disabilities.

Includes:

  • Visual accessibility
  • Audio accessibility
  • Motor accessibility
  • Cognitive accessibility

docs.title: Colorblind Modes
docs.text: Adjusting UI color palettes for visibility.

docs.title: Subtitles
docs.text:
Text representation of audio.

Includes:

  • Speaker labels
  • Sound descriptions

docs.title: Input Remapping
docs.text: Allowing players to customize controls.

docs.title: Difficulty Accessibility
docs.text: Adjustable challenge systems.

docs.title: UI Framework
docs.text: System that renders UI elements.

docs.title: Widget
docs.text:
Individual UI element.

Examples:

  • Health bar widget
  • Inventory slot widget

docs.title: Layout System
docs.text: Rules controlling UI positioning.

docs.title: Anchoring
docs.text: UI positioning relative to screen edges.

docs.title: Scaling
docs.text: Adjusting UI size for resolution.

docs.title: Menu Flow
docs.text: How players move through UI screens.

docs.title: Friction
docs.text:
Anything slowing interaction unnecessarily.

Example:

Too many confirmation screens.

docs.title: Click Depth
docs.text:
Number of steps required to reach a function.

Lower = better UX.

docs.title: Dead Ends
docs.text: Menus with no clear return path.

docs.title: Feedback Loop
docs.text:
System response to player actions.

Includes:

  • Visual feedback
  • Audio feedback
  • Haptic feedback

docs.title: Haptics
docs.text:
Controller vibration feedback.

Used for:

  • Impact
  • Alerts
  • Environmental effects

docs.title: Confirmation Feedback
docs.text:
Signals that action succeeded.

Examples:

  • Button highlight
  • Sound cue
  • Animation response

docs.title: Error Feedback
docs.text:
Indicates invalid actions.

Examples:

  • Red flash
  • Error sound
  • Shake animation

docs.title: Onboarding
docs.text: Teaching players systems gradually.

docs.title: Progressive Disclosure
docs.text:
Revealing complexity over time.

Example:

New mechanics introduced slowly.

docs.title: Tutorialization
docs.text: Explicit teaching moments.

docs.title: Soft Tutorial
docs.text:
Learning through gameplay rather than instructions.

Example:

Level design teaches mechanics implicitly.

docs.title: Retention UX
docs.text: How UX affects player return rates.

docs.title: Time to Fun
docs.text:
Time until player engages with core gameplay.

Critical KPI in modern design.

docs.title: Drop-off Rate
docs.text: Where players quit.

docs.title: Engagement Friction
docs.text: Points where players hesitate or disengage.

docs.title: FPS UX
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Combat readability
  • Crosshair clarity
  • Enemy visibility

docs.title: RPG UX
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Inventory management
  • Skill trees
  • Quest tracking

docs.title: Strategy UX
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Information density
  • Unit control systems
  • Minimap clarity

docs.title: Mobile UX
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Touch accuracy
  • One-handed usability
  • Session length optimization

docs.title: Beginner Thinking
docs.text: “How do we display this information?”

docs.title: Intermediate Thinking
docs.text: “How do we organize this interface?”

docs.title: Senior Thinking
docs.text: “How do we reduce cognitive load while preserving system depth and player agency?”

docs.title: Expert Thinking
docs.text: “How do we design information systems that allow players to make correct decisions under time pressure without consciously processing all available data?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In professional game development:

UX is not about showing information. It is about controlling what the player understands in time to act on it.

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What “Production” Means in Game Development
docs.text:
Production is often misunderstood as “scheduling.”

In reality, production is:

The system that converts creative intent into shippable work across time, people, and constraints.

A senior production mindset is not:

  • “Are we on schedule?”

It is:

  • “Is the system producing usable output at a sustainable rate?”

Production is fundamentally about:

  • Flow of work
  • Risk management
  • Dependency resolution
  • Communication structure
  • Scope control

docs.title: Chapter 1: The Role of Production in a Studio
docs.text:
Production exists to solve a core problem:

Creative work does not naturally organize itself into predictable output.

Without production:

  • Teams duplicate work
  • Dependencies block progress
  • Scope expands uncontrollably
  • Deadlines fail

docs.title: Producer (Core Role)
docs.text:
A producer is responsible for:

  • Delivery of milestones
  • Coordination across departments
  • Risk tracking
  • Scope management

They do NOT typically:

  • Design systems
  • Write code
  • Create art

They manage flow of production, not content itself.

docs.title: Project Manager (PM)
docs.text:
Focus:

Execution tracking and schedule adherence.

Differences from Producer:

  • PM = timeline + task tracking
  • Producer = creative + production alignment

docs.title: Development Director
docs.text:
High-level leadership role overseeing:

  • Production strategy
  • Cross-team alignment
  • Long-term planning

docs.title: Product Owner
docs.text:
More common in agile environments.

Focus:

  • Defining feature priority
  • Representing product vision
  • Managing backlog value

docs.title: Milestone
docs.text:
A major checkpoint in development.

Examples:

  • Prototype complete
  • Vertical slice complete
  • Alpha
  • Beta

Milestones are used for:

  • Publisher reviews
  • Funding releases
  • Internal validation

docs.title: Gate
docs.text:
A decision point where progress is approved or rejected.

Example:

“We will not proceed to Beta until performance targets are met.”

docs.title: Deliverable
docs.text:
A tangible output.

Examples:

  • Playable build
  • Art asset pack
  • Design document

docs.title: Dependency
docs.text:
A task that must be completed before another can start.

Example:

  • Animation system required before combat tuning

docs.title: Blocker
docs.text:
Something preventing progress entirely.

Example:

  • Critical bug preventing builds from running

docs.title: Risk
docs.text:
Anything that could threaten delivery.

Types:

  • Technical risk
  • Scope risk
  • Schedule risk
  • Creative risk

docs.title: Agile Development
docs.text:
Iterative development methodology focused on:

  • Flexibility
  • Incremental progress
  • Continuous feedback

docs.title: Scrum
docs.text:
A structured agile framework.

Key elements:

  • Sprints
  • Backlog
  • Stand-ups

docs.title: Sprint
docs.text: A fixed time period (usually 1–3 weeks) where work is completed.

docs.title: Backlog
docs.text:
A prioritized list of work items.

Includes:

  • Features
  • Bugs
  • Tasks

docs.title: Velocity
docs.text:
Amount of work a team completes per sprint.

Used for forecasting.

docs.title: Burndown Chart
docs.text: Visual tracking of remaining work over time.

docs.title: Stand-up
docs.text:
Daily team meeting.

Focus:

  • What was done
  • What will be done
  • What is blocked

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: Total amount of work required to complete a project.

docs.title: Scope Creep
docs.text:
Uncontrolled expansion of scope.

Example:

  • Adding features without adjusting time or budget

docs.title: Feature Creep
docs.text:
Adding unnecessary features.

Often causes:

  • Delays
  • Complexity explosion

docs.title: Scope Lock
docs.text:
Point where new features are no longer added.

Used to stabilize production.

docs.title: MVP (Minimum Viable Product)
docs.text:
Smallest playable version of a game.

Used for:

  • Testing core fun
  • Validating design

docs.title: Vertical Slice vs MVP
docs.text: Concept Purpose MVP Test core mechanics Vertical Slice Show final-quality vision

docs.title: Milestone Planning
docs.text: Structuring development into phases.

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text: High-level timeline of major deliverables.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text: Flow of work from concept to completion.

docs.title: Bottleneck
docs.text:
Stage slowing down entire pipeline.

Example:

  • Animation team overloaded blocking gameplay integration

docs.title: Critical Path
docs.text: Sequence of tasks that determines project duration.

docs.title: Resource Allocation
docs.text:
Assigning people to tasks.

Example:

  • 3 artists on environment
  • 2 engineers on gameplay

docs.title: Headcount
docs.text: Number of people on a team.

docs.title: Bandwidth
docs.text:
Available capacity of a team member.

Example:

  • “Artist is at 80% bandwidth”

docs.title: Burn Rate
docs.text:
How quickly money is being spent.

Critical in studios.

docs.title: Sync
docs.text: Cross-team alignment meeting.

docs.title: Cross-Discipline Review
docs.text: Meeting where design, art, and engineering align.

docs.title: Feedback Loop (Production Context)
docs.text:
Cycle of:

  • Build → Review → Adjust → Repeat

docs.title: Approval Chain
docs.text:
Sequence of sign-offs required before changes are accepted.

Example:

Designer → Lead → Director → Producer

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Written communication of systems.

Includes:

  • Design docs (GDD)
  • Technical specs
  • Art briefs

docs.title: GDD (Game Design Document)
docs.text:
Living document describing game systems.

Modern GDDs are often:

  • Modular
  • Iterative
  • Lightweight

Not massive static PDFs.

docs.title: TDD (Technical Design Document)
docs.text:
Describes system architecture.

Used by engineers.

docs.title: Art Brief
docs.text:
Instructions for asset creation.

Includes:

  • Style
  • Reference
  • Constraints
  • Function

docs.title: One-Pager
docs.text:
Short design pitch document.

Used for early concepts.

docs.title: Risk Register
docs.text: List of known risks and mitigation strategies.

docs.title: Mitigation
docs.text: Steps taken to reduce risk.

docs.title: Contingency Plan
docs.text: Backup plan if something fails.

docs.title: Technical Debt
docs.text:
Shortcuts in code or systems that must be fixed later.

Common phrase:

“We’ll pay that debt later.”

But often becomes permanent.

docs.title: Chapter 10: Production Reality in AAA vs Indie
AAA Production
docs.text:
Characteristics:

  • Heavy structure
  • Large teams
  • Formal approval chains
  • High documentation overhead

docs.title: Indie Production
docs.text:
Characteristics:

  • Flexible roles
  • Rapid iteration
  • Minimal bureaucracy
  • Faster decision cycles

docs.title: Live Service Production
docs.text:
Characteristics:

  • Continuous delivery
  • Seasonal content cycles
  • Analytics-driven decisions
  • Constant updates

docs.title: “On Track”
docs.text:
Means:

  • Current schedule is achievable

docs.title: “At Risk”
docs.text:
Means:

  • Likely delay unless mitigated

docs.title: “Blocked”
docs.text:
Means:

  • Work cannot proceed

docs.title: “Green / Yellow / Red Status”
docs.text:
Color-coded project health system:

  • Green = healthy
  • Yellow = caution
  • Red = critical issue

docs.title: Beginner Thinking
docs.text: “Are we finishing tasks?”

docs.title: Intermediate Thinking
docs.text: “Are we meeting deadlines?”

docs.title: Senior Thinking
docs.text: “Is the system of work sustainable, predictable, and aligned with creative goals?”

docs.title: Expert Thinking
docs.text: “How do we structure human effort so that unpredictable creative output becomes reliably shippable software under real-world constraints?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In professional studios:

Production is not bureaucracy — it is the nervous system of game development.

Without it:

  • Creativity collapses into chaos
  • Or rigidity collapses into stagnation

Good production enables both creativity and delivery simultaneously.

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What QA Actually Means
docs.text:
QA (Quality Assurance) is often misunderstood as:

“Finding bugs.”

In professional game development, QA is:

The systematic process of validating that a game is stable, consistent, reproducible, and compliant across all expected player behaviors and hardware environments.

A key AAA insight:

“A game is not finished when it works — it is finished when it cannot reasonably break.”

QA is not a final phase. It is a continuous feedback system embedded into development.

docs.title: Chapter 1: QA Philosophy in Game Development
Quality Assurance (QA)
docs.text:
The discipline responsible for:

  • Detecting defects
  • Verifying fixes
  • Ensuring stability
  • Validating player experience consistency

docs.title: Quality Control (QC)
docs.text:
Subset of QA focused on:

  • Testing outputs
  • Verifying correctness of builds

docs.title: Test Case
docs.text:
A structured scenario used to validate functionality.

Example:

  • “Player dies in lava → respawn at checkpoint”

docs.title: Test Suite
docs.text: Collection of test cases covering a system.

docs.title: Regression Testing
docs.text: Testing that previously fixed issues have not returned.

docs.title: Why regression testing matters
docs.text:
Games are highly interconnected systems.

Fixing one issue often breaks another.

docs.title: Bug
docs.text: Any unintended behavior.

docs.title: Defect
docs.text: Formal term used in production tracking systems.

docs.title: Issue
docs.text: General umbrella term (bug, task, improvement).

docs.title: Repro Steps (Reproduction Steps)
docs.text:
Exact steps to reliably trigger a bug.

Example:

1. Start mission2. Equip weapon3. Enter combat4. Reload → crash occurs

docs.title: Severity
docs.text: How bad the bug is technically.

docs.title: Priority
docs.text: How urgently it must be fixed.

docs.title: S1 — Critical
docs.text:
• Game crashes
  • Save corruption
  • Progress blocking

docs.title: S2 — Major
docs.text: • Gameplay broken but not blocking

docs.title: S3 — Minor
docs.text:
• Visual glitches
  • UI issues

docs.title: S4 — Cosmetic
docs.text: • Slight animation or text issues

docs.title: P0
docs.text: Fix immediately.

docs.title: P1
docs.text: Fix soon.

docs.title: P2
docs.text: Fix when possible.

docs.title: P3
docs.text: Backlog.

docs.title: Edge Case
docs.text:
Rare or extreme scenario not typical in normal gameplay.

Example:

  • Player exploits physics to leave map bounds

docs.title: Corner Case
docs.text: Very specific condition where multiple systems interact unexpectedly.

docs.title: Playtesting
docs.text:
Observing real players interacting with the game.

Focus:

  • Confusion points
  • Difficulty spikes
  • Unintended behavior

docs.title: Internal Testing
docs.text: Developers testing their own systems.

docs.title: External Testing
docs.text: Players outside the development team (often early access or focus groups).

docs.title: Smoke Testing
docs.text: Quick validation that the build launches and core systems function.

docs.title: Stress Testing
docs.text:
Pushing systems beyond expected limits.

Example:

  • Hundreds of enemies on screen
  • Extreme network load

docs.title: Load Testing
docs.text: Measuring performance under heavy usage conditions.

docs.title: Compatibility Testing
docs.text:
Ensuring the game runs on different:

  • Hardware
  • Consoles
  • Drivers
  • Operating systems

docs.title: Build
docs.text: A compiled playable version of the game.

docs.title: Internal Build
docs.text: Used by developers only.

docs.title: External Build
docs.text: Used by testers or players.

docs.title: Nightly Build
docs.text: Automated daily build from latest code.

docs.title: Candidate Build (RC)
docs.text: Potential release version.

docs.title: Gold Master (GM)
docs.text: Final approved release build.

docs.title: Patch
docs.text: Post-release update.

docs.title: Hotfix
docs.text: Urgent patch for critical issues.

docs.title: Update vs Patch
docs.text:
• Patch = fixes
  • Update = fixes + content changes

docs.title: Certification
docs.text:
Approval process required by platform holders before release.

Example platforms:

  • PlayStation
  • Xbox
  • Nintendo
  • Steam (less strict but still regulated)

docs.title: TRC (Technical Requirement Checklist)
docs.text:
Platform-specific rules a game must follow.

Example:

  • No crashes during suspend/resume
  • Proper save handling

docs.title: Compliance Testing
docs.text: Ensuring game meets platform rules.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text: Process of sending a build to platform for approval.

docs.title: Failure in Certification
docs.text:
Common causes:

  • Crash bugs
  • Save corruption
  • UI blocking input
  • System violations

docs.title: Crash
docs.text: Game or application terminates unexpectedly.

docs.title: Freeze
docs.text: Game stops responding but does not crash.

docs.title: Hang
docs.text: Similar to freeze; system stuck in infinite loop or blocked state.

docs.title: Null Reference Exception
docs.text: Trying to access non-existent object.

docs.title: Memory Leak
docs.text: Memory not released after use, causing performance degradation.

docs.title: Race Condition
docs.text:
Timing-dependent bug in concurrent systems.

Example:

Two systems updating same variable simultaneously.

docs.title: Desync
docs.text: Multiplayer state mismatch between clients and server.

docs.title: Frame Drop
docs.text: Sudden decrease in FPS.

docs.title: Stutter
docs.text: Irregular frame pacing causing jitter.

docs.title: Hitching
docs.text: Brief freeze due to asset loading or CPU spikes.

docs.title: Frame Time Spike
docs.text: Sudden increase in time needed to render a frame.

docs.title: Bottleneck
docs.text: System limiting overall performance.

docs.title: Overhead
docs.text: Extra computational cost not directly tied to gameplay.

docs.title: Bug Database
docs.text:
Central system tracking all issues.

Common tools:

  • Jira
  • Hansoft
  • Trello (smaller teams)

docs.title: Ticket
docs.text: Individual bug or task entry.

docs.title: Status Flow
docs.text:
Typical states:

New → Assigned → In Progress → Fixed → Verified → Closed

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: QA confirms fix is correct.

docs.title: Reopen
docs.text: Bug returns after being marked fixed.

docs.title: Bug Density
docs.text: Number of bugs per system or area.

docs.title: Escape Rate
docs.text: Bugs that reach players after release.

docs.title: Test Coverage
docs.text: Percentage of system tested.

docs.title: Stability Metrics
docs.text: Crash rate, freeze rate, etc.

docs.title: Build Verification Test (BVT)
docs.text: Ensures build is stable enough for testing.

docs.title: Continuous Integration Testing
docs.text: Automated testing during development.

docs.title: Automated Testing
docs.text:
Scripts simulate gameplay behavior.

Used for:

  • Regression checks
  • Repeated validation

docs.title: UX Bug
docs.text:
Confusing or misleading experience, not a technical failure.

Example:

  • Players don’t understand UI icon meaning

docs.title: Gameplay Bug
docs.text: Mechanics not functioning as intended.

docs.title: Visual Bug
docs.text: Graphical issues.

docs.title: Audio Bug
docs.text: Missing or incorrect sound playback.

docs.title: Soft Lock
docs.text:
Player cannot progress but game still runs.

Example:

Quest breaks leaving player stuck.

docs.title: Hard Lock
docs.text: Game becomes completely unresponsive.

docs.title: Beginner Thinking
docs.text: “Does the game have bugs?”

docs.title: Intermediate Thinking
docs.text: “Where are the bugs and how do we fix them?”

docs.title: Senior Thinking
docs.text: “What systemic patterns in design, architecture, or production are generating instability?”

docs.title: Expert Thinking
docs.text: “How do we design systems that are inherently testable, observable, and resilient under unpredictable player behavior?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In professional studios:

QA is not the end of development — it is the feedback system that keeps the entire development process grounded in reality.

Without QA:

  • Design assumptions fail
  • Systems drift into instability
  • Player experience becomes unpredictable

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What “Live Service” Actually Means
docs.text:
Live service is often misunderstood as:

“Updating the game after launch.”

In professional terms, it is:

A continuously operated product system where gameplay, content, and economy are iteratively adjusted based on player behavior data, retention modeling, and business performance signals.

A key AAA framing:

“A live game is never finished — it is continuously optimized for engagement, retention, and long-term value.”

This introduces a major shift:

  • From static design
  • To adaptive systems driven by data

docs.title: The Core Loop of Live Service Games
docs.text:
Acquire Players → Engage Players → Measure Behavior → Adjust Systems → Retain Players → Repeat

Every live service game operates on this cycle.

docs.title: Acquisition
docs.text:
Getting players into the game.

Sources:

  • Marketing
  • Social media
  • Platform featuring
  • Influencers

docs.title: Engagement
docs.text:
Keeping players active.

Mechanisms:

  • Daily rewards
  • Events
  • Progression systems
  • Social systems

docs.title: Retention
docs.text:
Ensuring players return over time.

Measured across:

  • Days
  • Weeks
  • Months

docs.title: Monetization
docs.text: Converting engagement into revenue.

docs.title: Analytics
docs.text: The measurement of player behavior through data.

docs.title: DAU (Daily Active Users)
docs.text: Number of unique players per day.

docs.title: MAU (Monthly Active Users)
docs.text: Unique players per month.

docs.title: DAU/MAU Ratio
docs.text:
Measures engagement intensity.

Higher ratio = more daily engagement.

docs.title: Retention Rate
docs.text:
Percentage of players returning after a period.

Common measures:

  • D1 retention (next day)
  • D7 retention
  • D30 retention

docs.title: Churn
docs.text:
Players leaving the game.

Opposite of retention.

docs.title: Session Length
docs.text: Average time spent per play session.

docs.title: Sessions per Day
docs.text: How often players return daily.

docs.title: Stickiness
docs.text:
Informal term for engagement quality.

Often approximated by:

DAU / MAU

docs.title: Monetization
docs.text: How a game generates revenue.

docs.title: Premium Model
docs.text:
One-time purchase.

Example:

  • Traditional console games

docs.title: Free-to-Play (F2P)
docs.text: Game is free, monetized through optional spending.

docs.title: Hybrid Model
docs.text: Combination of premium + in-game purchases.

docs.title: Microtransactions
docs.text: Small purchases within a game.

docs.title: Cosmetic Monetization
docs.text:
Selling visual changes only:

  • Skins
  • Emotes
  • Costumes

Important design principle:

Avoid pay-to-win imbalance.

docs.title: Pay-to-Win (P2W)
docs.text:
When purchases give gameplay advantage.

Highly controversial.

docs.title: Battle Pass
docs.text:
Progression-based monetization system.

Structure:

  • Earn rewards through gameplay
  • Premium track unlocks extra rewards

docs.title: Gacha System
docs.text:
Randomized reward monetization.

Common in:

  • Mobile games
  • Some RPG systems

Based on probability mechanics.

docs.title: Loot Box
docs.text:
Randomized reward container.

Similar to gacha but terminology varies by region.

docs.title: Whale (Industry Term)
docs.text:
High-spending player.

A small percentage of players generating large revenue share.

docs.title: ARPU (Average Revenue Per User)
docs.text: Revenue per player.

docs.title: ARPPU (Average Revenue Per Paying User)
docs.text: Revenue per paying user only.

docs.title: LTV (Lifetime Value)
docs.text: Total predicted revenue from a player over time.

docs.title: In-Game Economy
docs.text: System of currency, rewards, and sinks.

docs.title: Currency Sink
docs.text:
Mechanism that removes currency from the economy.

Examples:

  • Upgrades
  • Repairs
  • Crafting costs

Prevents inflation.

docs.title: Currency Faucet
docs.text:
Mechanism that introduces currency.

Examples:

  • Loot drops
  • Rewards
  • Quests

docs.title: Inflation (Game Economy)
docs.text: When currency value decreases due to oversupply.

docs.title: Resource Scarcity
docs.text: Limiting resources to create meaningful decisions.

docs.title: Reward Loop
docs.text: System that reinforces engagement through rewards.

docs.title: Retention Design
docs.text: Systems designed to keep players returning.

docs.title: Daily Login Reward
docs.text: Encourages daily engagement.

docs.title: Daily Quests
docs.text: Rotating objectives.

docs.title: Weekly Challenges
docs.text: Longer-term engagement structure.

docs.title: FOMO (Fear of Missing Out)
docs.text: Design strategy leveraging limited-time content.

docs.title: Seasonal Content
docs.text:
Time-limited updates:

  • Seasons
  • Events
  • Ranked resets

docs.title: LiveOps
docs.text:
Ongoing management of a live game.

Includes:

  • Events
  • Balance updates
  • Content drops
  • Economy adjustments

docs.title: Content Pipeline
docs.text: Workflow for producing and deploying updates.

docs.title: Patch Cycle
docs.text:
Regular update schedule.

Example:

  • Weekly patch
  • Monthly update
  • Seasonal release

docs.title: Hotfix
docs.text: Emergency patch for critical issues.

docs.title: Event Design
docs.text:
Temporary gameplay experiences.

Examples:

  • Holiday events
  • Limited modes
  • Boss events

docs.title: Cohort
docs.text:
Group of players sharing a characteristic.

Example:

  • Players who joined in Week 1

docs.title: Cohort Analysis
docs.text:
Tracking behavior of groups over time.

Used for:

  • Retention trends
  • Monetization behavior

docs.title: Segmentation
docs.text:
Dividing players by behavior type.

Examples:

  • Casual players
  • Competitive players
  • Paying users

docs.title: Behavioral Funnels
docs.text:
Tracking progression through systems.

Example:

Install → Tutorial → First Match → First Win → First Purchase

docs.title: Ethical Monetization
docs.text:
Designing systems that:

  • Avoid manipulation
  • Respect player agency
  • Maintain fairness

docs.title: Dark Patterns (Negative Term)
docs.text:
Design techniques that exploit psychology unfairly.

Examples:

  • Hidden costs
  • Forced urgency
  • Obscured probabilities

docs.title: Paywall
docs.text: Blocking content behind payment.

docs.title: Content Gating
docs.text: Restricting access based on progression or payment.

docs.title: Meta
docs.text:
Dominant strategies in competitive gameplay.

Example:

“Meta weapon build”

docs.title: Balance Patch
docs.text: Update adjusting game systems.

docs.title: Nerf
docs.text: Weakening a system.

docs.title: Buff
docs.text: Strengthening a system.

docs.title: Power Creep
docs.text:
Gradual increase in power over time.

Common in live games.

docs.title: KPI (Key Performance Indicator)
docs.text:
Core performance metric.

Examples:

  • Retention
  • Revenue
  • Engagement

docs.title: A/B Testing
docs.text:
Comparing two versions of a system.

Example:

Different UI layouts tested for conversion.

docs.title: Conversion Rate
docs.text:
Percentage of players who complete a desired action.

Example:

Purchase conversion rate.

docs.title: Funnel Drop-Off
docs.text: Where players stop progressing.

docs.title: Chapter 11: Senior Live Service Mental Model
Beginner Thinking
docs.text: “How do we add more content?”

docs.title: Intermediate Thinking
docs.text: “How do we keep players engaged longer?”

docs.title: Senior Thinking
docs.text: “How do we design systems that sustain long-term engagement while maintaining player trust and economic stability?”

docs.title: Expert Thinking
docs.text: “How do we balance psychological engagement loops, economic systems, and content cadence to create a self-sustaining player ecosystem without degrading player perception or fairness?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In live service development:

The game is no longer just a product — it is a continuously evolving behavioral system.

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What “The Industry” Actually Is
docs.text:
Beginners often think the game industry is:

“People making games.”

Professionally, it is:

A multi-layered economic ecosystem of studios, publishers, investors, platform holders, outsourcing networks, and middleware providers coordinating risk, capital, production capacity, and market distribution.

A key AAA truth:

“Most game development decisions are not creative decisions — they are risk and capital allocation decisions.”

docs.title: Chapter 1: The Industry Ecosystem Map
docs.text:
At a high level, the industry is composed of:

Platform Holders    ↓Publishers    ↓Studios    ↓Co-Development / Outsourcing    ↓Middleware / Tooling Providers

Each layer has different incentives.

docs.title: Indie Studio
docs.text:
Definition:

Small team, independent funding or self-funded development.

Characteristics:

  • High creative freedom
  • Limited budget
  • High risk tolerance
  • Fast iteration

Common tradeoff:

Freedom vs stability

docs.title: AA Studio
docs.text:
Definition:

Mid-sized studio between indie and AAA.

Characteristics:

  • Moderate budgets
  • Publisher relationships
  • Hybrid creative/production structure

docs.title: AAA Studio
docs.text:
Definition:

Large-scale production teams backed by major publishers.

Characteristics:

  • Hundreds to thousands of developers
  • High production value
  • Strong pipeline structure
  • Heavy specialization

Tradeoff:

Scale vs agility

docs.title: First-Party Studio
docs.text:
Owned by platform holder.

Examples:

  • Sony Interactive Entertainment studios
  • Xbox Game Studios

Advantages:

  • Platform support
  • Funding stability

docs.title: Third-Party Studio
docs.text: Independent or publisher-funded studio.

docs.title: Live Service Studio
docs.text:
Focused on continuously operating games.

Characteristics:

  • Data-driven development
  • Seasonal content pipelines
  • Long-term monetization focus

docs.title: Publisher
docs.text:
Entity responsible for:

  • Funding development
  • Marketing
  • Distribution
  • Platform relationships
  • Risk absorption

docs.title: Publishing Agreement
docs.text:
Contract between studio and publisher defining:

  • Budget
  • Revenue share
  • Milestones
  • IP ownership

docs.title: IP (Intellectual Property)
docs.text:
Ownership of game world/brand.

Example:

  • Who owns the franchise?

docs.title: Royalty
docs.text: Percentage of revenue paid to studio or publisher.

docs.title: Recoupment
docs.text: Publisher recovers investment before profit sharing begins.

docs.title: Self-Funded
docs.text:
Studio finances development independently.

Risk:

  • High financial exposure

docs.title: Publisher-Funded
docs.text:
Publisher finances production.

Tradeoff:

  • Less creative control

docs.title: Venture Capital (VC)
docs.text:
Investment from funds expecting growth and return.

Common in:

  • Mobile
  • Live service startups

docs.title: Early Access Funding
docs.text: Players fund development via early access purchases.

docs.title: Outsourcing
docs.text:
External teams produce specific assets or systems.

Examples:

  • Art production
  • Animation
  • QA testing

docs.title: Co-Development
docs.text:
External studio contributes directly to core game development.

More integrated than outsourcing.

docs.title: Vendor
docs.text:
External service provider.

Examples:

  • Motion capture studios
  • Audio studios

docs.title: Pipeline Fragmentation
docs.text:
When work is split across multiple companies.

Risk:

  • Communication loss
  • Inconsistent quality

docs.title: Middleware
docs.text:
External software integrated into game engine.

Examples:

  • Physics engines
  • Audio systems (FMOD, Wwise)
  • Networking libraries

docs.title: Engine Licensing
docs.text:
Using third-party engine:

  • Unreal Engine
  • Unity

Tradeoff:

  • Speed vs control

docs.title: Proprietary Engine
docs.text:
Studio-built engine.

Advantages:

  • Full control
  • Optimization freedom

Disadvantages:

  • High maintenance cost

docs.title: Budget
docs.text: Total funding allocated to project.

docs.title: Burn Rate
docs.text: How quickly money is spent monthly.

docs.title: Headcount Scaling
docs.text:
Increasing team size over time.

Important insight:

More people does not always mean faster development.

docs.title: Productivity Curve
docs.text:
Non-linear relationship between team size and output.

Large teams introduce:

  • Communication overhead
  • Coordination complexity

docs.title: Chapter 8: Risk Economics in Game Development
Development Risk
docs.text: Game may not be fun or viable.

docs.title: Technical Risk
docs.text: Engine or systems may not support design goals.

docs.title: Market Risk
docs.text: Game may not succeed commercially.

docs.title: Production Risk
docs.text: Game may not ship on time.

docs.title: Risk Mitigation Strategy
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Prototyping
  • Vertical slices
  • Scope reduction
  • Parallel development

docs.title: Platform Holder
docs.text:
Company controlling distribution platforms.

Examples:

  • Sony
  • Microsoft
  • Nintendo
  • Valve

docs.title: Platform Fee
docs.text:
Revenue share taken by platform holder.

Commonly:

  • 30% standard cut (varies by platform)

docs.title: Certification Gatekeeping
docs.text: Platforms enforce quality and compliance standards before release.

docs.title: Genre Positioning
docs.text:
How a game is categorized:

  • FPS
  • RPG
  • Strategy
  • Simulation

docs.title: Market Segment
docs.text:
Target audience group:

  • Hardcore gamers
  • Casual players
  • Mobile users

docs.title: Differentiation
docs.text: What makes a game unique in the market.

docs.title: Competitive Landscape
docs.text: Other games competing for same audience.

docs.title: Benchmarks
docs.text:
Reference games used for comparison.

Example:

“We are targeting Destiny-level engagement.”

docs.title: Live Service Strategy
docs.text: Long-term engagement model.

docs.title: Premium Strategy
docs.text: One-time purchase focus.

docs.title: Franchise Strategy
docs.text: Building long-term intellectual property.

docs.title: Portfolio Strategy
docs.text: Publisher balancing multiple games.

docs.title: Risk Diversification
docs.text: Spreading investment across multiple projects.

docs.title: ROI (Return on Investment)
docs.text: Profit relative to cost.

docs.title: P&L (Profit and Loss)
docs.text: Financial performance statement.

docs.title: EBITDA
docs.text: Financial performance metric excluding certain costs.

docs.title: Forecasting
docs.text: Predicting future revenue and performance.

docs.title: Greenlight
docs.text: Approval to begin full production.

docs.title: Cancellation
docs.text: Stopping development due to risk or failure.

docs.title: Developer Level
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Systems
  • Features
  • Tasks

docs.title: Lead Level
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Team coordination
  • Quality standards

docs.title: Director Level
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Vision alignment
  • Cross-discipline decisions

docs.title: Executive Level
docs.text:
Focus:

  • Financial performance
  • Strategic direction
  • Portfolio management

docs.title: Principle 1: Creativity is constrained by capital
docs.text: More funding → more scope But also more oversight

docs.title: Principle 2: Time is the most expensive resource
docs.text: Delays cost more than features.

docs.title: Principle 3: Communication overhead scales non-linearly
docs.text: More people = more complexity.

docs.title: Principle 4: Risk is always being redistributed
docs.text:
Every decision shifts risk somewhere:

  • To production
  • To technology
  • To market

docs.title: Beginner Thinking
docs.text: “What game should we make?”

docs.title: Intermediate Thinking
docs.text: “How do we fund and build this game?”

docs.title: Senior Thinking
docs.text: “How do we structure creative, technical, and financial risk so that the project remains viable from concept to launch?”

docs.title: Expert Thinking
docs.text: “How do we align incentives across developers, publishers, platforms, and players so that creative output, financial sustainability, and production reality converge into a shippable system?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In professional game development:

Studios do not simply make games — they operate within a network of financial, technical, and organizational constraints that shape what games are even possible.

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What “Creative Direction” Actually Is
docs.text:
A common misconception is that creative direction means:

“Having good ideas and making things look cool.”

In professional game development, it is:

The disciplined orchestration of multiple creative disciplines (design, art, audio, narrative, UX, tech constraints) into a unified experiential intention that remains consistent across an entire interactive system.

A key AAA truth:

“Creative direction is not expression alone — it is constraint management for emotional intent.”

docs.title: Chapter 1: The Role of the Creative Director
Creative Director (CD)
docs.text:
Responsible for:

  • Establishing the creative vision
  • Maintaining tonal consistency
  • Resolving cross-discipline conflicts
  • Defining aesthetic + emotional targets

They do NOT just “approve art.”

They:

Translate abstract emotional intent into enforceable production constraints.

docs.title: Creative Vision
docs.text:
The guiding idea of the entire game.

Examples:

  • “Survival horror grounded in psychological vulnerability”
  • “Power fantasy with escalating absurdity”
  • “Minimalist exploration with emotional ambiguity”

docs.title: Tone
docs.text:
The emotional “color” of experience.

Examples:

  • Somber
  • Playful
  • Tense
  • Heroic
  • Surreal

docs.title: Pillars (Design + Creative Pillars)
docs.text:
Core principles that guide all decisions.

Example:

  • “Player agency first”
  • “Readable combat clarity”
  • “Environmental storytelling over exposition”

docs.title: Chapter 2: Narrative Theory Language in Games
Narrative Design
docs.text:
Structuring story within interactive systems.

Not just writing dialogue.

docs.title: Dramatic Structure
docs.text:
Traditional story arcs applied to games:

  • Setup
  • Escalation
  • Climax
  • Resolution

docs.title: Three-Act Structure
docs.text:
Common narrative framework:

  • Act 1: Setup
  • Act 2: Conflict
  • Act 3: Resolution

docs.title: Character Arc
docs.text: Emotional transformation of a character over time.

docs.title: Player Agency
docs.text: Degree of control player has over narrative outcomes.

docs.title: Ludonarrative Alignment
docs.text:
When gameplay and story reinforce each other.

Example:

  • Stealth mechanics reinforcing fear and vulnerability

docs.title: Ludonarrative Dissonance
docs.text:
When gameplay contradicts narrative.

Example:

  • Story says “you are powerless”
  • Gameplay makes player unstoppable

docs.title: Behavioral Design
docs.text:
Designing systems that influence player behavior.

Includes:

  • Reward loops
  • Habit formation
  • Engagement systems

docs.title: Motivation Theory
docs.text: Why players engage:

docs.title: Intrinsic Motivation
docs.text:
• Fun
  • Curiosity
  • Mastery

docs.title: Extrinsic Motivation
docs.text:
• Rewards
  • Progression
  • Unlocks

docs.title: Flow State
docs.text:
Optimal engagement zone:

  • High challenge
  • High skill match

Coined by psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi.

docs.title: Cognitive Bias
docs.text:
Systematic patterns in human thinking.

Used in design:

  • Loss aversion
  • Sunk cost fallacy
  • Reward anticipation bias

docs.title: Dopamine Loop (Informal Industry Term)
docs.text:
Behavioral reinforcement cycle:

  • Action → Reward → Expectation → Repeat

docs.title: Chapter 4: Film Language in Game Direction
Cinematography
docs.text: Visual storytelling through camera control.

docs.title: Framing
docs.text: What is included/excluded in view.

docs.title: Composition
docs.text:
Arrangement of visual elements.

Includes:

  • Rule of thirds
  • Balance
  • Focus hierarchy

docs.title: Blocking
docs.text: Positioning characters in space for storytelling.

docs.title: Mise-en-scène
docs.text:
Everything visible in a scene:

  • Lighting
  • Props
  • Characters
  • Environment

docs.title: Editing Principles
docs.text:
Control of pacing through:

  • Cuts
  • Transitions
  • Timing

docs.title: Shot Composition in Games
docs.text:
Games simulate cinematic language dynamically:

  • Camera control
  • FOV shifts
  • Cutscenes vs gameplay transitions

docs.title: Spatial Composition
docs.text: How space guides emotion and behavior.

docs.title: Environmental Psychology
docs.text:
How environments affect player emotion.

Examples:

  • Tight corridors → tension
  • Open spaces → freedom

docs.title: Scale Design
docs.text: Manipulating perceived size.

docs.title: Verticality
docs.text: Use of height to influence gameplay dynamics.

docs.title: Landmarking
docs.text: Using memorable structures for navigation.

docs.title: Spatial Storytelling
docs.text:
Telling narrative through environment alone.

Example:

  • Broken weapons scattered → past conflict implied

docs.title: Emotional Scoring
docs.text: Music designed to reinforce emotional state.

docs.title: Leitmotif
docs.text: Recurring musical theme tied to character or idea.

docs.title: Diegetic Music
docs.text:
Music existing inside the game world.

Example:

  • Radio playing in environment

docs.title: Non-Diegetic Music
docs.text: Background soundtrack not part of world.

docs.title: Dynamic Music Systems
docs.text: Music that changes based on gameplay state.

docs.title: Color Theory
docs.text:
Emotional meaning of colors:

  • Red → danger / intensity
  • Blue → calm / sadness
  • Green → safety / nature

docs.title: Contrast
docs.text:
Difference between visual elements.

Used for:

  • Focus
  • Readability

docs.title: Composition Balance
docs.text: Visual stability of a scene.

docs.title: Visual Hierarchy
docs.text: Ordering of visual importance.

docs.title: Shape Language
docs.text:
Emotional meaning of shapes:

  • Sharp → danger
  • Round → friendly
  • Angular → aggressive

docs.title: Silhouette Readability
docs.text:
Recognizability of shape without detail.

Critical for:

  • Character design
  • Enemy recognition

docs.title: Gestalt Principles
docs.text:
Human perception rules:

  • Proximity
  • Similarity
  • Continuity
  • Closure

docs.title: Systems Integration Thinking
docs.text:
Combining:

  • Design systems
  • Visual language
  • Audio feedback
  • Narrative intent

Into one coherent experience.

docs.title: Creative Constraints
docs.text:
Limitations that improve creativity:

  • Engine limitations
  • Budget constraints
  • Time constraints

docs.title: Intent Alignment
docs.text: Ensuring all disciplines serve the same emotional goal.

docs.title: Experience Pillars
docs.text: High-level emotional and gameplay goals.

docs.title: Chapter 9: Creative Direction Language in Practice
“Make it feel…"
docs.text:
Common but vague phrase.

Professional translation:

  • Designers → mechanics
  • Artists → visuals
  • Audio → emotional tone
  • Engineers → system behavior

docs.title: “Juice”
docs.text:
Informal term for:

Micro-feedback that increases perceived responsiveness

Examples:

  • Screen shake
  • Hit flash
  • Sound feedback

docs.title: “Game Feel”
docs.text:
Subjective responsiveness of interaction.

Includes:

  • Input latency
  • Animation timing
  • Feedback intensity

docs.title: “Fantasy Fulfillment”
docs.text:
Delivering imagined player role experience.

Example:

  • “Be a powerful wizard”
  • “Be a tactical commander”

docs.title: Chapter 10: Senior Creative Direction Mental Model
Beginner Thinking
docs.text: “What should this game look like?”

docs.title: Intermediate Thinking
docs.text: “How do all parts of the game fit together?”

docs.title: Senior Thinking
docs.text: “How do I ensure every system consistently communicates the intended emotional experience?”

docs.title: Expert Thinking
docs.text: “How do I design a multi-system interactive experience where mechanics, aesthetics, narrative, and feedback all converge into a coherent emotional model under player-driven variability?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In professional game development:

Creative direction is not about generating ideas — it is about ensuring every system reinforces a single, coherent emotional truth under interactive conditions.

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What Communication Actually Means in Game Development
docs.text:
In early development, communication is often assumed to mean:

“Explaining ideas clearly.”

In professional game development, communication is:

The real-time translation of intent between incompatible specialist languages under production constraints.

A critical AAA truth:

“Most production failures are not technical failures — they are translation failures between disciplines.”

Each discipline speaks a different “language of reality”:

  • Designers speak in systems and player behavior
  • Artists speak in perception and visual intent
  • Engineers speak in constraints and architecture
  • Audio speaks in time and emotion
  • Producers speak in schedules and risk

docs.title: The Same Idea, Multiple Languages
docs.text: Example concept: “A powerful sword attack”

docs.title: Designer interpretation
docs.text:
• Damage values
  • Cooldown
  • Hitbox behavior
  • Risk/reward balance

docs.title: Programmer interpretation
docs.text:
• Animation state machine
  • Collision detection timing
  • Input buffering
  • Frame data

docs.title: Artist interpretation
docs.text:
• Silhouette clarity
  • Motion readability
  • Impact visual effects
  • Character posing

docs.title: Audio interpretation
docs.text:
• Impact intensity
  • Frequency layering
  • Timing synchronization

docs.title: Producer interpretation
docs.text:
• Time cost
  • Asset requirements
  • Risk impact on schedule

Same idea. Five incompatible translations.

docs.title: Creative Intent
docs.text:
High-level idea:

“This attack should feel heavy and decisive.”

docs.title: System Design Translation
docs.text:
• Slow startup
  • High damage
  • Commitment frames
  • Strong animation anticipation

docs.title: Technical Implementation
docs.text:
• Animation state machine timing
  • Hitbox activation windows
  • Input locking rules

docs.title: Presentation Layer
docs.text:
• Camera shake
  • Sound impact
  • Particle effects
  • Motion blur

docs.title: Production Constraint Layer
docs.text:
• Time budget
  • Performance cost
  • Asset reuse limitations

docs.title: Designers Think In:
docs.text:
• Player experience
  • System behavior
  • Abstract rules

docs.title: Programmers Think In:
docs.text:
• Data structures
  • State transitions
  • Performance constraints

docs.title: Designer says:
docs.text: “The dodge should feel responsive and forgiving.”

docs.title: Programmer translates:
docs.text:
• Input buffer window = X ms
  • Cancel frames = allowed during attack animation
  • State priority = dodge overrides attack
  • Latency compensation logic required

docs.title: Key Communication Terms
docs.text:
• “State machine”
  • “Frame window”
  • “Input buffering”
  • “Edge case handling”

docs.title: Artists Think In:
docs.text:
• Emotion
  • Shape language
  • Composition
  • Mood

docs.title: Technical Artists Think In:
docs.text:
• Engine constraints
  • Shader behavior
  • Memory usage
  • Pipeline limitations

docs.title: Artist:
docs.text: “This armor should feel ethereal and lightweight.”

docs.title: Technical Art Translation:
docs.text:
• Transparent shader with subsurface scattering
  • Reduced geometric density
  • Floating particle systems
  • Animated material emissive layer

docs.title: Common Breakdown Point
docs.text:
Artists assume:

“If it looks right, it is right.”

Engineers require:

“If it performs within budget, it is valid.”

docs.title: Producers Think In:
docs.text:
• Time
  • Scope
  • Risk
  • Resources

docs.title: Key Producer Questions
docs.text:
• How long will this take?
  • What dependencies does it have?
  • What breaks if this slips?
  • What is the fallback plan?

docs.title: Designer:
docs.text: “We should add a dynamic weather system.”

docs.title: Producer:
docs.text:
• Scope increase?
  • Engineering cost?
  • Art asset impact?
  • Performance risk?
  • Timeline impact?

docs.title: Production Terms
docs.text:
• “Scope impact”
  • “Dependency chain”
  • “Critical path”
  • “Resource allocation”

docs.title: Directors Think In:
docs.text:
• Emotional coherence
  • System alignment
  • Experience consistency

docs.title: Director Translation Layer
docs.text:
They unify:

  • Design intent
  • Visual direction
  • Audio mood
  • Narrative tone

docs.title: Example Directive:
docs.text:
“The player should feel hunted but never helpless.”

Becomes:

  • Design: limited resources but escape options
  • AI: pressure without guaranteed kills
  • Audio: tension layering
  • Visuals: obscured sightlines

docs.title: 1. “Feels Good” Problem
docs.text:
Phrase:

“Make it feel better.”

Issue:

No measurable definition.

Fix:

Translate into:

  • Input latency target
  • Animation timing windows
  • Feedback intensity levels

docs.title: 2. “Just Make It Work”
docs.text:
Usually hides:

  • Undefined requirements
  • Missing constraints
  • Unrealistic expectations

docs.title: 3. “Small Feature”
docs.text:
Often implies:

  • Large hidden system cost
  • Multiple dependencies

docs.title: 4. “Easy Change”
docs.text:
Usually means:

“Unknown complexity in downstream systems”

docs.title: Design Document (GDD)
docs.text: Attempts to unify intent across disciplines.

docs.title: Tech Spec
docs.text: Engineer-readable system definition.

docs.title: Art Brief
docs.text: Visual intent + constraints.

docs.title: Audio Brief
docs.text: Emotional + mechanical sound requirements.

docs.title: Jira Ticket
docs.text:
Atomic unit of work tracking.

Each document is:

A translation layer between incompatible cognitive models.

docs.title: Sync Meeting
docs.text:
Short alignment check:

  • Progress
  • Blockers
  • Dependencies

docs.title: Cross-Discipline Review
docs.text:
Ensures:

  • Systems coherence
  • Visual consistency
  • Technical feasibility

docs.title: Playtest Review
docs.text:
Shared observation of player behavior.

Often reveals:

Communication failures as gameplay failures.

docs.title: Beginner Thinking
docs.text: “Did I explain my idea clearly?”

docs.title: Intermediate Thinking
docs.text: “Did other people understand my idea?”

docs.title: Senior Thinking
docs.text: “Did my idea survive translation into other disciplines without distortion?”

docs.title: Expert Thinking
docs.text: “How do I encode intent so that it remains stable across design, engineering, art, audio, and production constraints without relying on verbal interpretation?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In professional studios:

Communication is not transmission — it is continuous translation between incompatible systems of expertise.

docs.title: Chapter 14 — Industry Jargon, Slang & Acronyms Dictionary
The Core Mental Shift: What “Jargon” Actually Does
docs.text:
Industry jargon is often seen as “informal slang.”

In reality, it is:

A compression system for complex production meaning into fast, shared signals that reduce communication cost in high-pressure environments.

A senior studio insight:

“Jargon exists because full explanations are too slow for real-time production.”

However, jargon also has a downside:

  • It can hide ambiguity
  • It can mask misunderstanding
  • It can create false confidence in alignment

docs.title: Chapter 1: Why Jargon Evolves in Game Studios
docs.text:
Game development is:

  • Fast-moving
  • Cross-disciplinary
  • Highly iterative
  • Constantly ambiguous

So language evolves to:

  • Reduce explanation time
  • Encode shared assumptions
  • Signal urgency or risk
  • Compress entire systems into short phrases

docs.title: KPI (Key Performance Indicator)
docs.text:
Metrics used to evaluate success.

Examples:

  • Retention
  • Revenue
  • Engagement

docs.title: DAU / MAU
docs.text:
• DAU = Daily Active Users
  • MAU = Monthly Active Users

docs.title: LTV (Lifetime Value)
docs.text: Expected total revenue per player.

docs.title: ARPU / ARPPU
docs.text:
• ARPU = Average Revenue Per User
  • ARPPU = Average Revenue Per Paying User

docs.title: MVP (Minimum Viable Product)
docs.text: Smallest playable version of a system or game.

docs.title: POC (Proof of Concept)
docs.text: Test to validate feasibility.

docs.title: RC (Release Candidate)
docs.text: Potential final build.

docs.title: GM (Gold Master)
docs.text: Final shipped version.

docs.title: WIP (Work In Progress)
docs.text: Incomplete asset or system.

docs.title: ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival)
docs.text: When something is expected to be completed.

docs.title: TBD / TBA
docs.text:
• To Be Determined
  • To Be Announced

docs.title: “Scope creep”
docs.text: Uncontrolled expansion of features.

docs.title: “Feature creep”
docs.text: Adding unnecessary features that dilute focus.

docs.title: “Ship it”
docs.text: Final decision to release.

docs.title: “Polish pass”
docs.text: Final refinement stage improving quality.

docs.title: “Jank”
docs.text:
Informal term for awkward or unpolished behavior in gameplay or animation.

Example:

  • Character sliding slightly during idle animation

docs.title: “Juice”
docs.text:
Enhancements that improve game feel:

  • Screen shake
  • Hit flash
  • Sound feedback

docs.title: “Spaghetti code”
docs.text: Unstructured, tightly coupled code that is hard to maintain.

docs.title: “Hard lock”
docs.text: Game becomes completely unresponsive.

docs.title: “Soft lock”
docs.text: Player cannot progress but game still runs.

docs.title: “Edge case”
docs.text: Rare scenario not normally expected.

docs.title: “Hacky solution”
docs.text: Quick fix that bypasses proper architecture.

docs.title: “Hotfix”
docs.text: Emergency patch deployed quickly.

docs.title: “Refactor”
docs.text: Rewriting code structure without changing behavior.

docs.title: “Tech debt”
docs.text: Accumulated cost of shortcuts in code.

docs.title: “Brittle system”
docs.text: System that breaks easily when modified.

docs.title: “Race condition”
docs.text: Timing-based bug in concurrent systems.

docs.title: “Memory leak”
docs.text: Memory not properly released.

docs.title: “Null ref”
docs.text: Null reference error.

docs.title: “Frame spike”
docs.text: Sudden performance drop.

docs.title: “Game feel”
docs.text: Overall tactile responsiveness of gameplay.

docs.title: “Readability”
docs.text: How clearly game state is communicated.

docs.title: “Signposting”
docs.text: Guiding player attention through design.

docs.title: “Skill ceiling”
docs.text: Maximum mastery potential.

docs.title: “Skill floor”
docs.text: Minimum skill required to engage.

docs.title: “Onboarding”
docs.text: Early learning phase for players.

docs.title: “Core loop”
docs.text: Primary repeated gameplay cycle.

docs.title: “Meta”
docs.text: Dominant strategies or systems outside core gameplay loop.

docs.title: “Balance pass”
docs.text: Adjusting systems for fairness.

docs.title: “Nerf / Buff”
docs.text:
• Nerf = weaken system
  • Buff = strengthen system

docs.title: “Placeholder”
docs.text: Temporary asset.

docs.title: “Greybox”
docs.text: Basic level layout without final art.

docs.title: “Blockout”
docs.text: Early level shape testing phase.

docs.title: “Kitbash”
docs.text: Reusing asset pieces to build new structures.

docs.title: “Silhouette readability”
docs.text: Clarity of shape without detail.

docs.title: “Tween”
docs.text: In-between animation frames.

docs.title: “Rig”
docs.text: Skeleton structure for animation.

docs.title: “IK / FK”
docs.text:
• IK = Inverse Kinematics
  • FK = Forward Kinematics

docs.title: “LOD”
docs.text: Level of Detail system.

docs.title: “Stinger”
docs.text: Short audio cue for events.

docs.title: “Mix pass”
docs.text: Balancing audio levels.

docs.title: “Diegetic”
docs.text: Sound that exists in game world.

docs.title: “Non-diegetic”
docs.text: Sound outside game world (UI, music).

docs.title: “Occlusion”
docs.text: Sound blocked by environment.

docs.title: “RTPC”
docs.text: Real-time parameter control.

docs.title: “Repro”
docs.text: Steps to reproduce a bug.

docs.title: “Can’t repro”
docs.text: Unable to consistently trigger bug.

docs.title: “Regression”
docs.text: Bug reappears after fix.

docs.title: “Blocker”
docs.text: Issue preventing progress.

docs.title: “Showstopper”
docs.text: Critical issue preventing release.

docs.title: “Triage”
docs.text: Prioritizing bugs.

docs.title: “Whale”
docs.text: High-spending player.

docs.title: “Dolphin”
docs.text: Moderate spender.

docs.title: “F2P”
docs.text: Free-to-play user.

docs.title: “Retention drop”
docs.text: Loss of returning players.

docs.title: “Content cadence”
docs.text: Rate of new content release.

docs.title: “Engagement spike”
docs.text: Sudden increase in player activity.

docs.title: “Meta shift”
docs.text: Change in dominant strategies or systems.

docs.title: “Greenlight”
docs.text: Approval to proceed.

docs.title: “At risk”
docs.text: Likely delay or failure.

docs.title: “Blocked”
docs.text: Work cannot continue.

docs.title: “WIP build”
docs.text: Unfinished playable version.

docs.title: “Drop-in”
docs.text: Small unplanned addition.

docs.title: “Scope hit”
docs.text: Feature increased development cost.

docs.title: Chapter 11: Hidden Meaning in Common Phrases
“It’s just a small change”
docs.text:
Often means:

Unknown complexity in multiple systems

docs.title: “Should be quick”
docs.text:
Usually indicates:

Risk is not fully understood

docs.title: “It works on my machine”
docs.text: Indicates environment mismatch or hidden dependency.

docs.title: “We’ll fix it later”
docs.text:
Often becomes:

Permanent technical debt

docs.title: “Feels off”
docs.text: Vague UX or timing issue requiring analysis.

docs.title: Beginner Thinking
docs.text: “What does this term mean?”

docs.title: Intermediate Thinking
docs.text: “What is this person trying to communicate?”

docs.title: Senior Thinking
docs.text: “What system complexity, risk, or constraint is being compressed into this shorthand?”

docs.title: Expert Thinking
docs.text: “What implicit assumptions, dependencies, and organizational pressures are encoded in this single phrase?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In real studios:

Jargon is not casual language — it is compressed system design communication under time pressure.

docs.title: The Core Mental Shift: What “Expert Thinking” Actually Is
docs.text:
At earlier stages, expertise is often assumed to mean:

“Knowing more terms, tools, or techniques.”

In professional game development, expert thinking is:

The ability to model interconnected systems (technical, creative, psychological, and organizational) simultaneously and reason about their emergent behavior under constraint.

A senior industry truth:

“Experts don’t think in features — they think in systems under pressure.”

docs.title: Chapter 1: Systems Thinking (The Foundation of Expertise)
Systems Thinking
docs.text:
Understanding how components interact as a whole rather than in isolation.

In games, everything is a system:

  • Combat system
  • Economy system
  • AI system
  • Animation system
  • UX system

docs.title: Key Principle
docs.text:
Changing one system always affects others.

Example:

Buffing player damage affects:

  • Enemy difficulty tuning
  • Economy pacing
  • Animation timing expectations
  • Audio feedback intensity
  • Player retention curves

docs.title: Feedback Loops
docs.text: Circular cause-and-effect relationships.

docs.title: Positive feedback loop
docs.text:
Amplifies behavior:

  • Reward → more play → more rewards → more play

docs.title: Negative feedback loop
docs.text:
Stabilizes behavior:

  • Difficulty increases → player slows down → balance stabilizes

docs.title: Emergent Behavior
docs.text:
Unexpected outcomes from simple rules interacting.

Example:

  • AI pathfinding + crowding = unpredictable combat behavior

docs.title: Mental Model of the Player
docs.text:
Experts do not design for “users.”

They design for:

probabilistic human behavior under cognitive load

docs.title: Attention Allocation
docs.text:
Players cannot process everything at once.

Designers must control:

  • What players notice
  • When they notice it
  • Why they care

docs.title: Decision Pressure
docs.text:
Time-sensitive decision-making situations.

Example:

  • Combat under fire
  • Resource scarcity
  • PvP encounters

docs.title: Cognitive Bandwidth
docs.text:
Limited mental capacity for processing information.

UX + combat design must respect this limit.

docs.title: Prediction Behavior
docs.text:
Players constantly predict:

  • Enemy actions
  • System outcomes
  • Reward timing

Designers exploit or support this.

docs.title: Creative Constraints
docs.text:
Limitations that guide design quality:

  • Engine limits
  • Budget limits
  • Time limits
  • Platform limitations

docs.title: Constraint as Design Tool
docs.text:
Experts do not avoid constraints — they use them:

“Constraints define the shape of creativity.”

docs.title: Scope as a Variable, Not a Constant
docs.text:
Beginner thinking:

“Scope is fixed.”

Expert thinking:

“Scope is continuously negotiated based on risk and value.”

docs.title: Tradeoff Reasoning
docs.text:
Every decision has cost:

  • Performance vs fidelity
  • Complexity vs clarity
  • Depth vs accessibility

docs.title: Emergent Design
docs.text: Designing simple systems that create complex outcomes.

docs.title: Examples:
docs.text:
• Physics + combat → emergent gameplay situations
  • AI + environment → unpredictable encounters
  • Loot systems → unexpected player economies

docs.title: Control vs Emergence Spectrum
docs.text: Control Emergence Scripted events Systemic behavior Predictable outcomes Unpredictable outcomes

docs.title: Expert Goal
docs.text: Maximize meaningful emergence while maintaining design intent.

docs.title: Production Reality Awareness
docs.text:
Experts constantly evaluate:

  • What is feasible in time
  • What introduces risk
  • What scales poorly

docs.title: Hidden Complexity Detection
docs.text:
Experts instinctively identify:

  • Systems that will explode in complexity later
  • “Simple” features that require large infrastructure
  • Interdependencies that are not obvious

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
“Add co-op mode” implies:

  • Networking architecture
  • Sync systems
  • AI scaling
  • UI redesign
  • Performance doubling

docs.title: Performance Budgeting
docs.text:
Allocating CPU/GPU resources:

  • AI: X ms
  • Rendering: Y ms
  • Physics: Z ms

docs.title: Deterministic
docs.text:
Same input → same output

Used for:

  • Replays
  • Competitive multiplayer

docs.title: Non-deterministic
docs.text:
Variable output

Used for:

  • Animation variation
  • Procedural systems

docs.title: Latency Modeling
docs.text:
Experts simulate:

  • Input delay
  • Network lag
  • Frame timing variability

docs.title: Emotional Arc Design
docs.text:
Structuring player emotion over time:

  • Tension → release → escalation → payoff

docs.title: Pacing Design
docs.text:
Controlling rhythm of gameplay:

  • Combat intensity
  • Exploration downtime
  • Reward frequency

docs.title: Emotional Budgeting
docs.text:
Managing how often strong emotions are triggered.

Too frequent → desensitization Too rare → disengagement

docs.title: Unified Experience Model
docs.text:
Experts think:

“How do all systems collectively produce one coherent player experience?”

docs.title: System Alignment
docs.text:
Ensuring:

  • Mechanics support narrative
  • Audio supports UX clarity
  • Visuals support gameplay readability
  • Economy supports engagement loop

docs.title: Cross-System Failure Detection
docs.text:
Example:

  • UX says “player should feel powerful”
  • Combat says “player is weak”
  • Audio says “uncertain impact”
  • Result: contradiction → confusion

docs.title: Probabilistic Thinking
docs.text:
Nothing is certain:

  • Player behavior varies
  • Systems interact unpredictably
  • Market response is unknown

docs.title: Risk vs Reward Evaluation
docs.text:
Every feature evaluated by:

  • Cost
  • Impact
  • Risk
  • Dependency load

docs.title: Hypothesis-Driven Development
docs.text:
Experts treat features as hypotheses:

“If we implement X, we expect Y behavior.”

Then validate through playtesting/data.

docs.title: Surface-Level Debugging (Beginner)
docs.text: “What is broken?”

docs.title: System-Level Debugging (Intermediate)
docs.text: “Which system caused the issue?”

docs.title: Root-Cause Systemic Debugging (Expert)
docs.text: “Which interaction between systems created this failure mode, and why was it not predicted?”

docs.title: Designing the Designers’ System
docs.text:
Experts design:

  • Tools
  • Pipelines
  • Workflows

Not just gameplay.

docs.title: Tooling as Force Multiplier
docs.text: Better tools → faster iteration → better game

docs.title: Example:
docs.text: • Level editor quality directly affects level design quality

docs.title: Beginner Thinking
docs.text: “How do I build this feature?”

docs.title: Intermediate Thinking
docs.text: “How do I make this feature work well?”

docs.title: Senior Thinking
docs.text: “How does this feature interact with every other system in the game?”

docs.title: Expert Thinking
docs.text: “How do I design a stable, evolving, multi-system interactive ecosystem that produces predictable emotional outcomes under unpredictable human behavior?”

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
In professional game development:

Expertise is not knowledge accumulation — it is system-level perception across design, technology, production, and psychology simultaneously.

docs.title: A
docs.text: A/B Testing — Comparing two versions of a feature to measure player behavior differences. AI (Artificial Intelligence) — Systems controlling non-player behavior (often rule-based, not true intelligence). Alpha — Early playable phase where core systems exist but are unfinished. Animation State Machine — System controlling animation transitions based on player or AI state. ARPU / ARPPU — Revenue metrics per user / paying user. Asset Pipeline — Workflow for creating, exporting, and integrating game assets. __A_ Pathfinding_ — Algorithm for optimal path navigation in AI systems. Affordance — Visual or functional cues indicating interactivity. Audio Middleware* — External audio systems (e.g., FMOD, Wwise).

docs.title: B
docs.text: Bandwidth (Production) — Available working capacity of a developer/team. Behavior Tree — Hierarchical AI decision system. Beta — Late development stage focusing on stability and bug fixing. Bottleneck — System or person limiting production flow. Build — Compiled playable version of a game. Burndown Chart — Graph tracking remaining work over time. Buff — Increase in power or effectiveness of a system. Bug — Unintended system behavior. Baking (Art) — Precomputing lighting or detail into textures. Blocker — Issue preventing progress. Branching (Git) — Parallel code development streams.

docs.title: C
docs.text: Camera Framing — Composition of visual focus in gameplay or cinematics. Cognitive Load — Mental effort required to process information. Collision Detection — System determining object intersections. Core Loop — Repeated gameplay cycle driving engagement. Critical Path — Sequence of tasks determining project timeline. Crunch — Excessive overtime during development (controversial). Culling (Occlusion) — Not rendering unseen objects. Client-Server Model — Multiplayer architecture with authoritative server. Cooldown — Time delay before ability reuse. Crafting System — Resource conversion mechanics for progression. Creative Direction — Unified vision guiding all disciplines.

docs.title: D
docs.text: DAU / MAU — Daily / Monthly Active Users. Data-Oriented Design (DOD) — Performance-focused programming model. Debugging — Process of identifying and fixing errors. Delta Time — Time between frames for consistent simulation. Design Pillars — Core principles guiding game design decisions. Dependency — Task or system required before another can function. Diegetic UI — UI existing within game world. Difficulty Curve — Progression of challenge over time. Draw Call — GPU instruction to render objects. DLC — Downloadable Content. Dissonance (Ludonarrative) — Conflict between gameplay and story.

docs.title: E
docs.text: Edge Case — Rare or extreme system condition. ECS (Entity Component System) — Data-driven architecture pattern. Emergent Gameplay — Unexpected outcomes from system interactions. Engagement Loop — Systems designed to retain players. Engine — Core software framework for game creation. Event System — Decoupled system communication model. Exploit — Unintended player advantage in system. Early Access — Public pre-release version for feedback/testing.

docs.title: F
docs.text: F2P (Free-to-Play) — Monetization model without upfront cost. Fidelity — Level of detail or realism. Frame Rate (FPS) — Number of rendered frames per second. Frame Time — Time taken to process a single frame. Friction (UX) — Barriers slowing user interaction. Foley — Recorded real-world sound effects. FOV (Field of View) — Camera viewing angle. Feedback Loop — System where output influences future input. Feature Creep — Uncontrolled feature expansion. Flow State — Optimal player engagement state. Funnel (Conversion) — Player progression through stages.

docs.title: G
docs.text: Game Loop — Continuous update cycle of game simulation. Gameplay Feel (“Game Feel”) — Responsiveness and tactile quality of interaction. Gacha — Randomized reward monetization system. GAAS (Games as a Service) — Continuous live-updated games. Gestalt Principles — Visual perception rules in design. GPU Bound — Performance limited by graphics processing. Gold Master (GM) — Final release version. Greenlight — Approval to begin full production.

docs.title: H
docs.text: Haptics — Vibration-based feedback. Headless System — System without visual representation (backend logic). Hitbox — Area used for detecting collisions in gameplay. HUD (Heads-Up Display) — On-screen gameplay information. Hotfix — Urgent patch for critical issues. HRTF — Audio system simulating human spatial hearing.

docs.title: I
docs.text: IK / FK — Inverse / Forward Kinematics in animation. Input Buffering — Allowing delayed input execution. Instance (Game Object) — Runtime object created from a template. Iteration — Repeated refinement cycle in development. Inventory System — Item management structure. IP (Intellectual Property) — Owned franchise or universe.

docs.title: J
docs.text: Jank — Unpolished or awkward system behavior. Juice — Enhanced feedback (shake, sound, effects). Jira Ticket — Task tracking entry in production systems.

docs.title: K
docs.text: KPI (Key Performance Indicator) — Success measurement metric. Kitbash — Reusing modular assets creatively.

docs.title: L
docs.text: Latency — Delay between input and response. LOD (Level of Detail) — Reduced complexity models at distance. LiveOps — Ongoing live game management. LTV (Lifetime Value) — Total revenue per player over time. Ludonarrative — Relationship between gameplay and story. Lightmap — Precomputed lighting texture.

docs.title: M
docs.text: MAU — Monthly Active Users. Meta (Game Meta) — Dominant strategies in gameplay. Middleware — External tools integrated into engine. Memory Leak — Unreleased memory causing performance issues. MVP (Minimum Viable Product) — Minimal playable system. Matchmaking — Player pairing system in multiplayer. Microtransaction — Small in-game purchase. Mise-en-scène — Visual arrangement in a scene.

docs.title: N
docs.text: NavMesh — Navigation system for AI movement. Netcode — Networking implementation for multiplayer. Nerf — Reduction in system power. Node System — Graph-based logic structure. Non-Diegetic UI — UI outside game world.

docs.title: O
docs.text: Occlusion Culling — Not rendering hidden objects. OOP (Object-Oriented Programming) — Class-based programming model. Onboarding — Early player learning experience. Open World — Non-linear exploration structure. Overhead — Extra system cost beyond core function.

docs.title: P
docs.text: Patch — Game update. Pacing — Control of gameplay rhythm. PBR (Physically Based Rendering) — Realistic material system. Pipeline — Workflow system for production. Playtest — Player testing session. Player Agency — Degree of control player has. POC (Proof of Concept) — Feasibility prototype. Postmortem — Analysis after project completion. Procedural Generation — System-generated content. Production — Phase of active game development. Profiling — Performance analysis process. P2W (Pay-to-Win) — Monetization giving gameplay advantage.

docs.title: Q
docs.text: QA (Quality Assurance) — Testing and validation process. QC (Quality Control) — Output verification testing. Quest System — Structured objective system.

docs.title: R
docs.text: Raycasting — Line-based collision detection. Repro Steps — Steps to reproduce a bug. Retention — Player return rate. Regression — Reappearing bug after fix. Rigidbody — Physics-simulated object. RTPC — Real-time audio parameter control.

docs.title: S
docs.text: Scope Creep — Uncontrolled feature expansion. Shader — GPU visual effect program. Silhouette Readability — Recognizability of shape. Skill Ceiling/Floor — Mastery range of a system. Spawn System — Entity generation logic. Stinger (Audio) — Short audio cue. State Machine — System with defined behavior states. Stickiness — Engagement metric. Sprint — Timeboxed development cycle. Steam/Platform Holder — Distribution platform provider. Sync (Meeting) — Cross-team alignment session.

docs.title: T
docs.text: Tech Debt — Accumulated inefficiencies in code/design. Tick Rate — Frequency of simulation updates. Telemetry — Player behavior data collection. TDD (Technical Design Document) — Engineering system spec. Triage — Bug prioritization process. Texture Mapping — Applying images to 3D surfaces.

docs.title: U
docs.text: UI (User Interface) — Visual interaction layer. UX (User Experience) — Overall interaction experience. Utility AI — AI decision system based on scoring.

docs.title: V
docs.text: VFX (Visual Effects) — Particle and visual simulation effects. Vertical Slice — Fully polished representative gameplay segment. Velocity (Scrum) — Work output per sprint. Voice Limiting — Restricting simultaneous audio sources.

docs.title: W
docs.text: Waypoint — Navigation marker in level design. WIP (Work In Progress) — Incomplete asset/system. Wwise / FMOD — Audio middleware tools.

docs.title: X / Y / Z (Advanced / Rare Terms)
docs.text: Z-Depth — Distance from camera in rendering. Zone Design — Structuring game world regions. Z-fighting — Rendering artifact from overlapping surfaces.

docs.title: FINAL NOTE (Professional Interpretation Layer)
docs.text:
This glossary is not just vocabulary.

In professional game development:

Each term is a compressed representation of systems, workflows, and constraints that only fully make sense inside a production environment.
